The {{ill|Seiryōden lightning strike incident|ja|清涼殿落雷事件}} further linked Michizane’s spirit with the thunder god. The court pardoned Michizane’s alleged crimes and posthumously elevated his rank. In Kyoto’s {{ill|Kitano|ja|北野 (京都市)}}, Karatanojin, a local {{ill|land kami|ja|地主神}}, was enshrined as part of {{ill|Kitano Tenmangū|ja|北野天満宮}} to appease Michizane’s spirit.<ref>Karatanojin is now enshrined at the Hi no Miko Shrine within Kitano Tenmangū.</ref> Similarly, in Dazaifu, Michizane was worshipped at a mausoleum later developed into {{ill|Dazaifu Tenmangū|ja|太宰府天満宮}} by order of Emperor Daigo and {{ill|Fujiwara no Nakahira|ja|藤原仲平}}. Additionally, {{ill|Osaka Tenmangū|ja|大阪天満宮}} was built in 949 after seven pine trees mysteriously sprouted overnight at a site believed to be under Michizane’s protection.
During the late Heian and Kamakura periods, the belief in Michizane’s wrath waned. According to the ''Tenjin Engi'' (天神縁起), he became revered as a merciful deity of integrity, poetry, and the arts, granting longevity in this life and bliss in the next.<ref>As Michizane was identified with the Eleven-Faced Kannon Bosatsu.</ref> Over time, Tenjin also became a protector against maritime disasters and was worshiped by royalty, military leaders, and merchants as a deity for victory and national protection. In the Edo period, Michizane was popularly venerated as the god of learning due to his scholarly achievements.
== Origin == {{ill|Kitano Tenmangū|ja|北野天満宮}} and {{ill|Dazaifu Tenmangū|ja|太宰府天満宮}} were established independently, each claiming to be the birthplace of Tenjin Faith. Others, such as {{ill|Hōfu Tenmangū|ja|防府天満宮}} and {{ill|Yoki Tenman Shrine|ja|與喜天満神社}}, also assert being the oldest origin sites.
== Local Tenjin Faith == [[Image:MATSUYAMA_CASTLE(IYO),TENJIN_YAGURA.JPG|thumb|right|250px|The Tenjin Yagura at Matsuyama Castle, Iyo Province]] [[Image:Japanese crest Kaga Umebachi.svg|thumb|right|150px|The Kaga Umebachi crest of the Maeda clan]]
; Hokuriku In {{ill|Fukui Prefecture|ja|福井県}} and {{ill|Toyama Prefecture|ja|富山県}}, Tenjin images are displayed in the alcove when a firstborn son is born. The tradition of offering flounder on January 25 is also observed. These practices were promoted by {{ill|Matsudaira Shungaku|ja|松平春嶽}} during the late Edo period.
== Tenjin and the Number 25 == Michizane’s birth and death anniversaries fall on the 25th of June and February, respectively, leading to monthly festivals on the 25th. This association persists even today with events like the {{ill|Hajime Tenjin|ja|始め天神}} on January 25 and the {{ill|Shimai Tenjin|ja|終い天神}} on December 25.
== Tenjin and Oxen == Numerous legends link Michizane with oxen, portraying them as his loyal protectors and companions. Due to these stories, oxen are considered messengers of the deity and are often depicted in Tenmangū shrines as reclining ox statues.

[[ja:%E5%A4%A9%E7%A5%9E%E4%BF%A1%E4%BB%B0]][[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ Unlinked]] [[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ Unlinked]] [[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:bump 1-20 (2)]] [[Category:dd]]

'''Tenjin Faith''' (天神信仰) refers to the {{ill|faith|ja|信仰}} in Tenjin, the {{ill|Raijin|ja|雷神}} (god of thunder) in {{ill|Japan|ja|日本}}. Specifically, it pertains to the {{ill|Shinto|ja|神道}} belief system venerating {{ill|Sugawara no Michizane|ja|菅原道真}} as "Tenjin-sama," revered as a deity for fear and prayer.
Originally, Tenjin referred to {{ill|Amatsukami|ja|天津神}} as opposed to {{ill|Kunitsukami|ja|国津神}}, and it was not the name of a specific deity. However, shortly after Michizane’s death, he was enshrined as the deity '''{{ill|Tenman Daijizai Tenjin|ja|天満大自在天神}}''' (てんまんだいじざいてんじん). Following the {{ill|Seiryōden lightning strike incident|ja|清涼殿落雷事件}}, Michizane’s vengeful spirit was associated with the local {{ill|Karatanojin|ja|火雷大神}}, leading to his recognition as '''Karatentenjin''' (火雷天神). Later, Karatanojin was incorporated as a retinue spirit under titles like '''Nihon Daijō Itokuten''' (日本太政威徳天 / にほんだじょういとくてん) and even '''Jitsudō Gongen''' (実道権現)<ref>Cited in the Tenjinkyō (天神経), transmitted through terakoya (寺子屋).</ref>. Michizane’s spirit was further venerated in Buddhist texts such as ''Watokō Tenjin'' (渡唐天神), ''Myōhō Tenjinkyō'' (妙法天神経), and ''Tenjinkyō'' (天神経).<ref>Initially, Tenjin faith was closely tied to Buddhism, as monks from both the Tendai and Shingon schools were involved in its development. Temples and pagodas were often built on shrine grounds until the {{ill|Haibutsu Kishaku|ja|廃仏毀釈}} movement during the Meiji era.</ref> This veneration became widely known as Tenjin Faith.
[[Image:Gagyu(sumatsunasiki tenmangu).JPG|thumb|right|270px|The reclining ox at Suma Tsunashiki Tenmangu, Kobe City]]
== History == Sugawara no Michizane was demoted and exiled to {{ill|Dazaifu|ja|大宰府}} due to the conspiracy of {{ill|Fujiwara no Tokihira|ja|藤原時平}}, dying there in despair.
After his death, Michizane’s vassal {{ill|Aji Sake Yasuyuki|ja|味酒安行}} enshrined him as the deity '''{{ill|Tenman Daijizai Tenjin|ja|天満大自在天神}}'''.
Subsequently, an outbreak of plagues, droughts, and the deaths of Emperor {{ill|Daigo|ja|醍醐天皇}}'s princes caused fear of Michizane’s curse. The {{ill|Seiryōden lightning strike incident|ja|清涼殿落雷事件}} further linked Michizane’s spirit with the thunder god. The court pardoned Michizane’s alleged crimes and posthumously elevated his rank. In Kyoto’s {{ill|Kitano|ja|北野 (京都市)}}, Karatanojin, a local {{ill|land kami|ja|地主神}}, was enshrined as part of {{ill|Kitano Tenmangū|ja|北野天満宮}} to appease Michizane’s spirit.<ref>Karatanojin is now enshrined at the Hi no Miko Shrine within Kitano Tenmangū.</ref> Similarly, in Dazaifu, Michizane was worshipped at a mausoleum later developed into {{ill|Dazaifu Tenmangū|ja|太宰府天満宮}} by order of Emperor Daigo and {{ill|Fujiwara no Nakahira|ja|藤原仲平}}. Additionally, {{ill|Osaka Tenmangū|ja|大阪天満宮}} was built in 949 after seven pine trees mysteriously sprouted overnight at a site believed to be under Michizane’s protection.
During the late Heian and Kamakura periods, the belief in Michizane’s wrath waned. According to the ''Tenjin Engi'' (天神縁起), he became revered as a merciful deity of integrity, poetry, and the arts, granting longevity in this life and bliss in the next.<ref>As Michizane was identified with the Eleven-Faced Kannon Bosatsu.</ref> Over time, Tenjin also became a protector against maritime disasters and was worshiped by royalty, military leaders, and merchants as a deity for victory and national protection. In the Edo period, Michizane was popularly venerated as the god of learning due to his scholarly achievements.
== Origin == {{ill|Kitano Tenmangū|ja|北野天満宮}} and {{ill|Dazaifu Tenmangū|ja|太宰府天満宮}} were established independently, each claiming to be the birthplace of Tenjin Faith. Others, such as {{ill|Hōfu Tenmangū|ja|防府天満宮}} and {{ill|Yoki Tenman Shrine|ja|與喜天満神社}}, also assert being the oldest origin sites.
== Local Tenjin Faith == [[Image:MATSUYAMA_CASTLE(IYO),TENJIN_YAGURA.JPG|thumb|right|250px|The Tenjin Yagura at Matsuyama Castle, Iyo Province]] [[Image:Japanese crest Kaga Umebachi.svg|thumb|right|150px|The Kaga Umebachi crest of the Maeda clan]]
; Hokuriku In {{ill|Fukui Prefecture|ja|福井県}} and {{ill|Toyama Prefecture|ja|富山県}}, Tenjin images are displayed in the alcove when a firstborn son is born. The tradition of offering flounder on January 25 is also observed. These practices were promoted by {{ill|Matsudaira Shungaku|ja|松平春嶽}} during the late Edo period.
== Tenjin and the Number 25 == Michizane’s birth and death anniversaries fall on the 25th of June and February, respectively, leading to monthly festivals on the 25th. This association persists even today with events like the {{ill|Hajime Tenjin|ja|始め天神}} on January 25 and the {{ill|Shimai Tenjin|ja|終い天神}} on December 25.
== Tenjin and Oxen == Numerous legends link Michizane with oxen, portraying them as his loyal protectors and companions. Due to these stories, oxen are considered messengers of the deity and are often depicted in Tenmangū shrines as reclining ox statues.

[[Category:Shinbutsu-shūgō]] [[Category:Shinto]] [[Category:Sugawara no Michizane]] [[Category:Tenmangū]] [[Category:Japanese folk beliefs]] [[Category:Spirit worship]] [[Category:Faith]] [[ja:天神信仰]]
[[ja:%E5%A4%A9%E7%A5%9E%E4%BF%A1%E4%BB%B0]][[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ Unlinked]] [[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ Unlinked]] [[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:bump 1-20 (2)]] [[Category:bump 1-25]]

'''Tenjin Faith''' (天神信仰) refers to the {{ill|faith|ja|信仰}} in Tenjin, the {{ill|Raijin|ja|雷神}} (god of thunder) in {{ill|Japan|ja|日本}}. Specifically, it pertains to the {{ill|Shinto|ja|神道}} belief system venerating {{ill|Sugawara no Michizane|ja|菅原道真}} as "Tenjin-sama," revered as a deity for fear and prayer.
Originally, Tenjin referred to {{ill|Amatsukami|ja|天津神}} as opposed to {{ill|Kunitsukami|ja|国津神}}, and it was not the name of a specific deity. However, shortly after Michizane’s death, he was enshrined as the deity '''{{ill|Tenman Daijizai Tenjin|ja|天満大自在天神}}''' (てんまんだいじざいてんじん). Following the {{ill|Seiryōden lightning strike incident|ja|清涼殿落雷事件}}, Michizane’s vengeful spirit was associated with the local {{ill|Karatanojin|ja|火雷大神}}, leading to his recognition as '''Karatentenjin''' (火雷天神). Later, Karatanojin was incorporated as a retinue spirit under titles like '''Nihon Daijō Itokuten''' (日本太政威徳天 / にほんだじょういとくてん) and even '''Jitsudō Gongen''' (実道権現)<ref>Cited in the Tenjinkyō (天神経), transmitted through terakoya (寺子屋).</ref>. Michizane’s spirit was further venerated in Buddhist texts such as ''Watokō Tenjin'' (渡唐天神), ''Myōhō Tenjinkyō'' (妙法天神経), and ''Tenjinkyō'' (天神経).<ref>Initially, Tenjin faith was closely tied to Buddhism, as monks from both the Tendai and Shingon schools were involved in its development. Temples and pagodas were often built on shrine grounds until the {{ill|Haibutsu Kishaku|ja|廃仏毀釈}} movement during the Meiji era.</ref> This veneration became widely known as Tenjin Faith.
[[Image:Gagyu(sumatsunasiki tenmangu).JPG|thumb|right|270px|The reclining ox at Suma Tsunashiki Tenmangu, Kobe City]]
== History == Sugawara no Michizane was demoted and exiled to {{ill|Dazaifu|ja|大宰府}} due to the conspiracy of {{ill|Fujiwara no Tokihira|ja|藤原時平}}, dying there in despair.
After his death, Michizane’s vassal {{ill|Aji Sake Yasuyuki|ja|味酒安行}} enshrined him as the deity '''{{ill|Tenman Daijizai Tenjin|ja|天満大自在天神}}'''.
Subsequently, an outbreak of plagues, droughts, and the deaths of Emperor {{ill|Daigo|ja|醍醐天皇}}'s princes caused fear of Michizane’s curse. The {{ill|Seiryōden lightning strike incident|ja|清涼殿落雷事件}} further linked Michizane’s spirit with the thunder god. The court pardoned Michizane’s alleged crimes and posthumously elevated his rank. In Kyoto’s {{ill|Kitano|ja|北野 (京都市)}}, Karatanojin, a local {{ill|land kami|ja|地主神}}, was enshrined as part of {{ill|Kitano Tenmangū|ja|北野天満宮}} to appease Michizane’s spirit.<ref>Karatanojin is now enshrined at the Hi no Miko Shrine within Kitano Tenmangū.</ref> Similarly, in Dazaifu, Michizane was worshipped at a mausoleum later developed into {{ill|Dazaifu Tenmangū|ja|太宰府天満宮}} by order of Emperor Daigo and {{ill|Fujiwara no Nakahira|ja|藤原仲平}}. Additionally, {{ill|Osaka Tenmangū|ja|大阪天満宮}} was built in 949 after seven pine trees mysteriously sprouted overnight at a site believed to be under Michizane’s protection.
During the late Heian and Kamakura periods, the belief in Michizane’s wrath waned. According to the ''Tenjin Engi'' (天神縁起), he became revered as a merciful deity of integrity, poetry, and the arts, granting longevity in this life and bliss in the next.<ref>As Michizane was identified with the Eleven-Faced Kannon Bosatsu.</ref> Over time, Tenjin also became a protector against maritime disasters and was worshiped by royalty, military leaders, and merchants as a deity for victory and national protection. In the Edo period, Michizane was popularly venerated as the god of learning due to his scholarly achievements.
== Origin == {{ill|Kitano Tenmangū|ja|北野天満宮}} and {{ill|Dazaifu Tenmangū|ja|太宰府天満宮}} were established independently, each claiming to be the birthplace of Tenjin Faith. Others, such as {{ill|Hōfu Tenmangū|ja|防府天満宮}} and {{ill|Yoki Tenman Shrine|ja|與喜天満神社}}, also assert being the oldest origin sites.
== Local Tenjin Faith == [[Image:MATSUYAMA_CASTLE(IYO),TENJIN_YAGURA.JPG|thumb|right|250px|The Tenjin Yagura at Matsuyama Castle, Iyo Province]] [[Image:Japanese crest Kaga Umebachi.svg|thumb|right|150px|The Kaga Umebachi crest of the Maeda clan]]
; Hokuriku In {{ill|Fukui Prefecture|ja|福井県}} and {{ill|Toyama Prefecture|ja|富山県}}, Tenjin images are displayed in the alcove when a firstborn son is born. The tradition of offering flounder on January 25 is also observed. These practices were promoted by {{ill|Matsudaira Shungaku|ja|松平春嶽}} during the late Edo period.
== Tenjin and the Number 25 == Michizane’s birth and death anniversaries fall on the 25th of June and February, respectively, leading to monthly festivals on the 25th. This association persists even today with events like the {{ill|Hajime Tenjin|ja|始め天神}} on January 25 and the {{ill|Shimai Tenjin|ja|終い天神}} on December 25.
== Tenjin and Oxen == Numerous legends link Michizane with oxen, portraying them as his loyal protectors and companions. Due to these stories, oxen are considered messengers of the deity and are often depicted in Tenmangū shrines as reclining ox statues.
== References == {{Reflist|2}}

Temples and pagodas were often built on shrine grounds until the {{ill|Haibutsu Kishaku|ja|廃仏毀釈}} movement during the Meiji era.</ref> This veneration became widely known as Tenjin Faith.
[[Image:Gagyu(sumatsunasiki tenmangu).JPG|thumb|right|270px|The reclining ox at Suma Tsunashiki Tenmangu, Kobe City]]
== History == Sugawara no Michizane was demoted and exiled to {{ill|Dazaifu|ja|大宰府}} due to the conspiracy of {{ill|Fujiwara no Tokihira|ja|藤原時平}}, dying there in despair.
After his death, Michizane’s vassal {{ill|Aji Sake Yasuyuki|ja|味酒安行}} enshrined him as the deity '''{{ill|Tenman Daijizai Tenjin|ja|天満大自在天神}}'''.
Subsequently, an outbreak of plagues, droughts, and the deaths of Emperor {{ill|Daigo|ja|醍醐天皇}}'s princes caused fear of Michizane’s curse. The {{ill|Seiryōden lightning strike incident|ja|清涼殿落雷事件}} further linked Michizane’s spirit with the thunder god. The court pardoned Michizane’s alleged crimes and posthumously elevated his rank. In Kyoto’s {{ill|Kitano|ja|北野 (京都市)}}, Karatanojin, a local {{ill|landlord deity|ja|地主神}}, was enshrined as part of {{ill|Kitano Tenmangū|ja|北野天満宮}} to appease Michizane’s spirit.<ref>Karatanojin is now enshrined at the Hi no Miko Shrine within Kitano Tenmangū.</ref> Similarly, in Dazaifu, Michizane was worshipped at a mausoleum later developed into {{ill|Dazaifu Tenmangū|ja|太宰府天満宮}} by order of Emperor Daigo and {{ill|Fujiwara no Nakahira|ja|藤原仲平}}. Additionally, {{ill|Osaka Tenmangū|ja|大阪天満宮}} was built in 949 after seven pine trees mysteriously sprouted overnight at a site believed to be under Michizane’s protection.
During the late Heian and Kamakura periods, the belief in Michizane’s wrath waned. According to the ''Tenjin Engi'' (天神縁起), he became revered as a merciful deity of integrity, poetry, and the arts, granting longevity in this life and bliss in the next.<ref>As Michizane was identified with the Eleven-Faced Kannon Bosatsu.</ref> Over time, Tenjin also became a protector against maritime disasters and was worshiped by royalty, military leaders, and merchants as a deity for victory and national protection. In the Edo period, Michizane was popularly venerated as the god of learning due to his scholarly achievements.
== Origin == {{ill|Kitano Tenmangū|ja|北野天満宮}} and {{ill|Dazaifu Tenmangū|ja|太宰府天満宮}} were established independently, each claiming to be the birthplace of Tenjin Faith. Others, such as {{ill|Hōfu Tenmangū|ja|防府天満宮}} and {{ill|Yoki Tenman Shrine|ja|與喜天満神社}}, also assert being the oldest origin sites.
== Local Tenjin Faith == [[Image:MATSUYAMA_CASTLE(IYO),TENJIN_YAGURA.JPG|thumb|right|250px|The Tenjin Yagura at Matsuyama Castle, Iyo Province]] [[Image:Japanese crest Kaga Umebachi.svg|thumb|right|150px|The Kaga Umebachi crest of the Maeda clan]]
; Hokuriku In {{ill|Fukui Prefecture|ja|福井県}} and {{ill|Toyama Prefecture|ja|富山県}}, Tenjin images are displayed in the alcove when a firstborn son is born. The tradition of offering flounder on January 25 is also observed. These practices were promoted by {{ill|Matsudaira Shungaku|ja|松平春嶽}} during the late Edo period.
== Tenjin and the Number 25 == Michizane’s birth and death anniversaries fall on the 25th of June and February, respectively, leading to monthly festivals on the 25th. This association persists even today with events like the {{ill|Hajime Tenjin|ja|始め天神}} on January 25 and the {{ill|Shimai Tenjin|ja|終い天神}} on December 25.
== Tenjin and Oxen == Numerous legends link Michizane with oxen, portraying them as his loyal protectors and companions. Due to these stories, oxen are considered messengers of the deity and are often depicted in Tenmangū shrines as reclining ox statues.
== References == {{Reflist|2}}

'''Tenjin Faith''' (天神信仰) refers to the {{ill|faith|ja|信仰}} in Tenjin, the {{ill|Raijin|ja|雷神}} (god of thunder) in {{ill|Japan|ja|日本}}. Specifically, it pertains to the {{ill|Shinto|ja|神道}} belief system venerating {{ill|Sugawara no Michizane|ja|菅原道真}} as "Tenjin-sama," revered as a deity for fear and prayer.
Originally, Tenjin referred to {{ill|Amatsukami|ja|天津神}} as opposed to {{ill|Kunitsukami|ja|国津神}}, and it was not the name of a specific deity. However, shortly after Michizane’s death, he was enshrined as the deity '''{{ill|Tenman Daijizai Tenjin|ja|天満大自在天神}}''' (てんまんだいじざいてんじん). Following the {{ill|Seiryōden lightning strike incident|ja|清涼殿落雷事件}}, Michizane’s vengeful spirit was associated with the local {{ill|Karatanojin|ja|火雷大神}}, leading to his recognition as '''Karatentenjin''' (火雷天神). Later, Karatanojin was incorporated as a retinue spirit under titles like '''Nihon Daijō Itokuten''' (日本太政威徳天 / にほんだじょういとくてん) and even '''Jitsudō Gongen''' (実道権現)<ref>Cited in the Tenjinkyō (天神経), transmitted through terakoya (寺子屋).</ref>. Michizane’s spirit was further venerated in Buddhist texts such as ''Watokō Tenjin'' (渡唐天神), ''Myōhō Tenjinkyō'' (妙法天神経), and ''Tenjinkyō'' (天神経).<ref>Initially, Tenjin faith was closely tied to Buddhism, as monks from both the Tendai and Shingon schools were involved in its development. Temples and pagodas were often built on shrine grounds until the {{ill|Haibutsu Kishaku|ja|廃仏毀釈}} movement during the Meiji era.</ref> This veneration became widely known as Tenjin Faith.
[[Image:Gagyu(sumatsunasiki tenmangu).JPG|thumb|right|270px|The reclining ox at Suma Tsunashiki Tenmangu, Kobe City]]
== History == Sugawara no Michizane was demoted and exiled to {{ill|Dazaifu|ja|大宰府}} due to the conspiracy of {{ill|Fujiwara no Tokihira|ja|藤原時平}}, dying there in despair.

Subsequently, an outbreak of plagues, droughts, and the deaths of Emperor {{ill|Daigo|ja|醍醐天皇}}'s princes caused fear of Michizane’s curse. The {{ill|Seiryōden lightning strike incident|ja|清涼殿落雷事件}} further linked Michizane’s spirit with the thunder god. The court pardoned Michizane’s alleged crimes and posthumously elevated his rank. In Kyoto’s {{ill|Kitano|ja|北野 (京都市)}}, Karatanojin, a local {{ill|landlord deity|ja|地主神}}, was enshrined as part of {{ill|Kitano Tenmangū|ja|北野天満宮}} to appease Michizane’s spirit.<ref>Karatanojin is now enshrined at the Hi no Miko Shrine within Kitano Tenmangū.</ref> Similarly, in Dazaifu, Michizane was worshipped at a mausoleum later developed into {{ill|Dazaifu Tenmangū|ja|太宰府天満宮}} by order of Emperor Daigo and {{ill|Fujiwara no Nakahira|ja|藤原仲平}}. Additionally, {{ill|Osaka Tenmangū|ja|大阪天満宮}} was built in 949 after seven pine trees mysteriously sprouted overnight at a site believed to be under Michizane’s protection.
During the late Heian and Kamakura periods, the belief in Michizane’s wrath waned. According to the ''Tenjin Engi'' (天神縁起), he became revered as a merciful deity of integrity, poetry, and the arts, granting longevity in this life and bliss in the next.<ref>As Michizane was identified with the Eleven-Faced Kannon Bosatsu.</ref> Over time, Tenjin also became a protector against maritime disasters and was worshiped by royalty, military leaders, and merchants as a deity for victory and national protection. In the Edo period, Michizane was popularly venerated as the god of learning due to his scholarly achievements.
== Origin == {{ill|Kitano Tenmangū|ja|北野天満宮}} and {{ill|Dazaifu Tenmangū|ja|太宰府天満宮}} were established independently, each claiming to be the birthplace of Tenjin Faith. Others, such as {{ill|Hōfu Tenmangū|ja|防府天満宮}} and {{ill|Yoki Tenman Shrine|ja|與喜天満神社}}, also assert being the oldest origin sites.
== Local Tenjin Faith == [[Image:MATSUYAMA_CASTLE(IYO),TENJIN_YAGURA.JPG|thumb|right|250px|The Tenjin Yagura at Matsuyama Castle, Iyo Province]] [[Image:Japanese crest Kaga Umebachi.svg|thumb|right|150px|The Kaga Umebachi crest of the Maeda clan]]
; Hokuriku In {{ill|Fukui Prefecture|ja|福井県}} and {{ill|Toyama Prefecture|ja|富山県}}, Tenjin images are displayed in the alcove when a firstborn son is born. The tradition of offering flounder on January 25 is also observed. These practices were promoted by {{ill|Matsudaira Shungaku|ja|松平春嶽}} during the late Edo period.
== Tenjin and the Number 25 == Michizane’s birth and death anniversaries fall on the 25th of June and February, respectively, leading to monthly festivals on the 25th. This association persists even today with events like the {{ill|Hajime Tenjin|ja|始め天神}} on January 25 and the {{ill|Shimai Tenjin|ja|終い天神}} on December 25.
== Tenjin and Oxen == Numerous legends link Michizane with oxen, portraying them as his loyal protectors and companions. Due to these stories, oxen are considered messengers of the deity and are often depicted in Tenmangū shrines as reclining ox statues.

== Origin == {{ill|Kitano Tenmangū|ja|北野天満宮}} and {{ill|Dazaifu Tenmangū|ja|太宰府天満宮}} were established independently, each claiming to be the birthplace of Tenjin Faith. Others, such as {{ill|Hōfu Tenmangū|ja|防府天満宮}} and {{ill|Yoki Tenman Shrine|ja|與喜天満神社}}, also assert being the oldest origin sites.
== Local Tenjin Faith == [[Image:MATSUYAMA_CASTLE(IYO),TENJIN_YAGURA.JPG|thumb|right|250px|The Tenjin Yagura at Matsuyama Castle, Iyo Province]] [[Image:Japanese crest Kaga Umebachi.svg|thumb|right|150px|The Kaga Umebachi crest of the Maeda clan]]
; Hokuriku In {{ill|Fukui Prefecture|ja|福井県}} and {{ill|Toyama Prefecture|ja|富山県}}, Tenjin images are displayed in the alcove when a firstborn son is born. The tradition of offering flounder on January 25 is also observed. These practices were promoted by {{ill|Matsudaira Shungaku|ja|松平春嶽}} during the late Edo period.
== Tenjin and the Number 25 == Michizane’s birth and death anniversaries fall on the 25th of June and February, respectively, leading to monthly festivals on the 25th. This association persists even today with events like the {{ill|Hajime Tenjin|ja|始め天神}} on January 25 and the {{ill|Shimai Tenjin|ja|終い天神}} on December 25.
== Tenjin and Oxen == Numerous legends link Michizane with oxen, portraying them as his loyal protectors and companions. Due to these stories, oxen are considered messengers of the deity and are often depicted in Tenmangū shrines as reclining ox statues.

[[ja:%E5%A4%A9%E7%A5%9E%E4%BF%A1%E4%BB%B0]][[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ Unlinked]] [[Category:pages needing attribution]][[Category:Tenjin faith]][[Category:pages needing attribution]] [[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:pages needing attribution]][[Category:Tenjin faith]][[Category:pages needing attribution]] [[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ Unlinked]] [[Category:pages needing attribution]][[Category:Tenjin faith]][[Category:pages needing attribution]] [[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:pages needing attribution]][[Category:Tenjin faith]][[Category:pages needing attribution]] [[Category:bump 1-20 (2)]]

'''Tenjin Faith''' (天神信仰) refers to the {{ill|faith|ja|信仰}} in Tenjin, the {{ill|Raijin|ja|雷神}} (god of thunder) in {{ill|Japan|ja|日本}}. Specifically, it pertains to the {{ill|Shinto|ja|神道}} belief system venerating {{ill|Sugawara no Michizane|ja|菅原道真}} as "Tenjin-sama," revered as a deity for fear and prayer.
Originally, Tenjin referred to {{ill|Amatsukami|ja|天津神}} as opposed to {{ill|Kunitsukami|ja|国津神}}, and it was not the name of a specific deity. However, shortly after Michizane’s death, he was enshrined as the deity '''{{ill|Tenjin (kami)|lt=Tenman Daijizai Tenjin|ja|天満大自在天神}}''' (てんまんだいじざいてんじん). Following the {{ill|Seiryōden lightning strike incident|ja|清涼殿落雷事件}}, Michizane’s vengeful spirit was associated with the local {{ill|Yakusanoikazuchi|ja|火雷大神}}, leading to his recognition as '''Karatentenjin''' (火雷天神). Later, Karatanojin was incorporated as a retinue spirit under titles like '''Nihon Daijō Itokuten''' (日本太政威徳天 / にほんだじょういとくてん) and even '''Jitsudō Gongen''' (実道権現)<ref>Cited in the Tenjinkyō (天神経), transmitted through terakoya (寺子屋).</ref>. Michizane’s spirit was further venerated in Buddhist texts such as ''Watokō Tenjin'' (渡唐天神), ''Myōhō Tenjinkyō'' (妙法天神経), and ''Tenjinkyō'' (天神経).<ref>Initially, Tenjin faith was closely tied to Buddhism, as monks from both the Tendai and Shingon schools were involved in its development. Temples and pagodas were often built on shrine grounds until the {{ill|Haibutsu Kishaku|ja|廃仏毀釈}} movement during the Meiji era.</ref> This veneration became widely known as Tenjin Faith.
[[Image:Gagyu(sumatsunasiki tenmangu).JPG|thumb|right|270px|The reclining ox at Suma Tsunashiki Tenmangu, Kobe City]]
== History == Sugawara no Michizane was demoted and exiled to {{ill|Dazaifu|ja|大宰府}} due to the conspiracy of {{ill|Fujiwara no Tokihira|ja|藤原時平}}, dying there in despair.
After his death, Michizane’s vassal {{ill|Aji Sake Yasuyuki|ja|味酒安行}} enshrined him as the deity '''{{ill|Tenjin (kami)|lt=Tenman Daijizai Tenjin|ja|天満大自在天神}}'''.
Subsequently, an outbreak of plagues, droughts, and the deaths of {{ill|Emperor Daigo|ja|醍醐天皇}}'s princes caused fear of Michizane’s curse. The {{ill|Seiryōden lightning strike incident|ja|清涼殿落雷事件}} further linked Michizane’s spirit with the thunder god. The court pardoned Michizane’s alleged crimes and posthumously elevated his rank. In Kyoto’s {{ill|Kitano|ja|北野 (京都市)}}, Karatanojin, a local {{ill|landlord deity|ja|地主神}}, was enshrined as part of {{ill|Kitano Tenmangū|ja|北野天満宮}} to appease Michizane’s spirit.<ref>Karatanojin is now enshrined at the Hi no Miko Shrine within Kitano Tenmangū.</ref> Similarly, in Dazaifu, Michizane was worshipped at a mausoleum later developed into {{ill|Dazaifu Tenmangū|ja|太宰府天満宮}} by order of Emperor Daigo and [[:en:Fujiwara_no_Nakahira|Fujiwara no Nakahira]]. Additionally, {{ill|Osaka Tenmangū|ja|大阪天満宮}} was built in 949 after seven pine trees mysteriously sprouted overnight at a site believed to be under Michizane’s protection.
During the late Heian and Kamakura periods, the belief in Michizane’s wrath waned. According to the ''Tenjin Engi'' (天神縁起), he became revered as a merciful deity of integrity, poetry, and the arts, granting longevity in this life and bliss in the next.<ref>As Michizane was identified with the Eleven-Faced Kannon Bosatsu.</ref> Over time, Tenjin also became a protector against maritime disasters and was worshiped by royalty, military leaders, and merchants as a deity for victory and national protection. In the Edo period, Michizane was popularly venerated as the god of learning due to his scholarly achievements.
== Origin == {{ill|Kitano Tenmangū|ja|北野天満宮}} and {{ill|Dazaifu Tenmangū|ja|太宰府天満宮}} were established independently, each claiming to be the birthplace of Tenjin Faith. Others, such as {{ill|Hōfu Tenmangū|ja|防府天満宮}} and {{ill|Yoki Tenman Shrine|ja|與喜天満神社}}, also assert being the oldest origin sites.
== Local Tenjin Faith == [[Image:MATSUYAMA_CASTLE(IYO),TENJIN_YAGURA.JPG|thumb|right|250px|The Tenjin Yagura at Matsuyama Castle, Iyo Province]] [[Image:Japanese crest Kaga Umebachi.svg|thumb|right|150px|The Kaga Umebachi crest of the Maeda clan]]
; Hokuriku In {{ill|Fukui Prefecture|ja|福井県}} and {{ill|Toyama Prefecture|ja|富山県}}, Tenjin images are displayed in the alcove when a firstborn son is born. The tradition of offering flounder on January 25 is also observed. These practices were promoted by {{ill|Matsudaira Shungaku|ja|松平春嶽}} during the late Edo period.
== Tenjin and the Number 25 == Michizane’s birth and death anniversaries fall on the 25th of June and February, respectively, leading to monthly festivals on the 25th. This association persists even today with events like the {{ill|Hajime Tenjin|ja|始め天神}} on January 25 and the {{ill|Shimai Tenjin|ja|終い天神}} on December 25.
== Tenjin and Oxen == Numerous legends link Michizane with oxen, portraying them as his loyal protectors and companions. Due to these stories, oxen are considered messengers of the deity and are often depicted in Tenmangū shrines as reclining ox statues.

[[ja:%E5%A4%A9%E7%A5%9E%E4%BF%A1%E4%BB%B0]][[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ Unlinked]] [[Category:pages needing attribution]][[Category:Tenjin faith]][[Category:pages needing attribution]] [[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:pages needing attribution]][[Category:Tenjin faith]][[Category:pages needing attribution]] [[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ Unlinked]] [[Category:pages needing attribution]][[Category:Tenjin faith]][[Category:pages needing attribution]] [[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:pages needing attribution]][[Category:Tenjin faith]][[Category:pages needing attribution]] [[Category:bump 1-20 (2)]]

'''Tenjin Faith''' (天神信仰) refers to the {{ill|faith|ja|信仰}} in Tenjin, the {{ill|Raijin|ja|雷神}} (god of thunder) in {{ill|Japan|ja|日本}}. Specifically, it pertains to the {{ill|Shinto|ja|神道}} belief system venerating {{ill|Sugawara no Michizane|ja|菅原道真}} as "Tenjin-sama," revered as a deity for fear and prayer.
Originally, Tenjin referred to {{ill|Amatsukami|ja|天津神}} as opposed to {{ill|Kunitsukami|ja|国津神}}, and it was not the name of a specific deity. However, shortly after Michizane’s death, he was enshrined as the deity '''{{ill|Tenjin (kami)|lt=Tenman Daijizai Tenjin|ja|天満大自在天神}}''' (てんまんだいじざいてんじん). Following the {{ill|Seiryōden lightning strike incident|ja|清涼殿落雷事件}}, Michizane’s vengeful spirit was associated with the local {{ill|Yakusanoikazuchi|ja|火雷大神}}, leading to his recognition as '''Karatentenjin''' (火雷天神). Later, Karatanojin was incorporated as a retinue spirit under titles like '''Nihon Daijō Itokuten''' (日本太政威徳天 / にほんだじょういとくてん) and even '''Jitsudō Gongen''' (実道権現)<ref>Cited in the Tenjinkyō (天神経), transmitted through terakoya (寺子屋).</ref>. Michizane’s spirit was further venerated in Buddhist texts such as ''Watokō Tenjin'' (渡唐天神), ''Myōhō Tenjinkyō'' (妙法天神経), and ''Tenjinkyō'' (天神経).<ref>Initially, Tenjin faith was closely tied to Buddhism, as monks from both the Tendai and Shingon schools were involved in its development. Temples and pagodas were often built on shrine grounds until the {{ill|Haibutsu Kishaku|ja|廃仏毀釈}} movement during the Meiji era.</ref> This veneration became widely known as Tenjin Faith.
[[Image:Gagyu(sumatsunasiki tenmangu).JPG|thumb|right|270px|The reclining ox at Suma Tsunashiki Tenmangu, Kobe City]]
== History == Sugawara no Michizane was demoted and exiled to {{ill|Dazaifu|ja|大宰府}} due to the conspiracy of {{ill|Fujiwara no Tokihira|ja|藤原時平}}, dying there in despair.
After his death, Michizane’s vassal {{ill|Aji Sake Yasuyuki|ja|味酒安行}} enshrined him as the deity '''{{ill|Tenjin (kami)|lt=Tenman Daijizai Tenjin|ja|天満大自在天神}}'''.
Subsequently, an outbreak of plagues, droughts, and the deaths of {{ill|Emperor Daigo|ja|醍醐天皇}}'s princes caused fear of Michizane’s curse. The {{ill|Seiryōden lightning strike incident|ja|清涼殿落雷事件}} further linked Michizane’s spirit with the thunder god. The court pardoned Michizane’s alleged crimes and posthumously elevated his rank. In Kyoto’s {{ill|Kitano|ja|北野 (京都市)}}, Karatanojin, a local {{ill|landlord deity|ja|地主神}}, was enshrined as part of {{ill|Kitano Tenmangū|ja|北野天満宮}} to appease Michizane’s spirit.<ref>Karatanojin is now enshrined at the Hi no Miko Shrine within Kitano Tenmangū.</ref> Similarly, in Dazaifu, Michizane was worshipped at a mausoleum later developed into {{ill|Dazaifu Tenmangū|ja|太宰府天満宮}} by order of Emperor Daigo and [[:en:Fujiwara_no_Nakahira|Fujiwara no Nakahira]]. Additionally, {{ill|Osaka Tenmangū|ja|大阪天満宮}} was built in 949 after seven pine trees mysteriously sprouted overnight at a site believed to be under Michizane’s protection.
During the late Heian and Kamakura periods, the belief in Michizane’s wrath waned. According to the ''Tenjin Engi'' (天神縁起), he became revered as a merciful deity of integrity, poetry, and the arts, granting longevity in this life and bliss in the next.<ref>As Michizane was identified with the Eleven-Faced Kannon Bosatsu.</ref> Over time, Tenjin also became a protector against maritime disasters and was worshiped by royalty, military leaders, and merchants as a deity for victory and national protection. In the Edo period, Michizane was popularly venerated as the god of learning due to his scholarly achievements.
== Origin == {{ill|Kitano Tenmangū|ja|北野天満宮}} and {{ill|Dazaifu Tenmangū|ja|太宰府天満宮}} were established independently, each claiming to be the birthplace of Tenjin Faith. Others, such as {{ill|Hōfu Tenmangū|ja|防府天満宮}} and {{ill|Yoki Tenman Shrine|ja|與喜天満神社}}, also assert being the oldest origin sites.
== Local Tenjin Faith == [[Image:MATSUYAMA_CASTLE(IYO),TENJIN_YAGURA.JPG|thumb|right|250px|The Tenjin Yagura at Matsuyama Castle, Iyo Province]] [[Image:Japanese crest Kaga Umebachi.svg|thumb|right|150px|The Kaga Umebachi crest of the Maeda clan]]
; Hokuriku In {{ill|Fukui Prefecture|ja|福井県}} and {{ill|Toyama Prefecture|ja|富山県}}, Tenjin images are displayed in the alcove when a firstborn son is born. The tradition of offering flounder on January 25 is also observed. These practices were promoted by [[Matsudaira Yoshinaga]] during the late Edo period.
== Tenjin and the Number 25 == Michizane’s birth and death anniversaries fall on the 25th of June and February, respectively, leading to monthly festivals on the 25th. This association persists even today with events like the {{ill|Hajime Tenjin|ja|始め天神}} on January 25 and the {{ill|Shimai Tenjin|ja|終い天神}} on December 25.
== Tenjin and Oxen == Numerous legends link Michizane with oxen, portraying them as his loyal protectors and companions. Due to these stories, oxen are considered messengers of the deity and are often depicted in Tenmangū shrines as reclining ox statues.
== References == {{Reflist|2}}
== Related Articles == * {{ill|Tenjin (kami)|lt=Tenman Daijizai Tenjin|ja|天満大自在天神}} * {{ill|Tenmangū|ja|天満宮}} * {{ill|Tenjin Matsuri|ja|天神祭}} * {{ill|Flying Plum|ja|飛梅}} * {{ill|Umegae Mochi|ja|梅ヶ枝餅}} * {{ill|Uso-kae|ja|鷽替え}} * {{ill|Kitano Ōchanoyu|ja|北野大茶会}} * {{ill|Japanese-Chinese Synthesis|ja|和魂漢才}} * {{ill|Umebachi|ja|梅鉢}}
== External Links == {{Shinto Portal|[[Image:Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|40px]]}} * [http://www.jinjahoncho.or.jp/2007/02/01%E3%80%80%E5%A4%A9%E7%A5%9E%E3%81%95%E3%81%BE/ Tenjin-sama - Jinja Honchō]{{Dead link|date=August 2024}} * [http://www.tokyo-jinjacho.or.jp/syoukai/21_itabashi/21021.html Kitano Shrine, Tokumaru, Itabashi Ward, Tokyo]

However, shortly after Michizane’s death, he was enshrined as the deity '''{{ill|Tenjin (kami)|lt=Tenman Daijizai Tenjin|ja|天満大自在天神}}''' (てんまんだいじざいてんじん). Following the {{ill|Seiryōden lightning strike incident|ja|清涼殿落雷事件}}, Michizane’s vengeful spirit was associated with the local {{ill|Yakusanoikazuchi|ja|火雷大神}}, leading to his recognition as '''Karatentenjin''' (火雷天神). Later, Karatanojin was incorporated as a retinue spirit under titles like '''Nihon Daijō Itokuten''' (日本太政威徳天 / にほんだじょういとくてん) and even '''Jitsudō Gongen''' (実道権現)<ref>Cited in the Tenjinkyō (天神経), transmitted through terakoya (寺子屋).</ref>. Michizane’s spirit was further venerated in Buddhist texts such as ''Watokō Tenjin'' (渡唐天神), ''Myōhō Tenjinkyō'' (妙法天神経), and ''Tenjinkyō'' (天神経).<ref>Initially, Tenjin faith was closely tied to Buddhism, as monks from both the Tendai and Shingon schools were involved in its development. Temples and pagodas were often built on shrine grounds until the {{ill|Haibutsu Kishaku|ja|廃仏毀釈}} movement during the Meiji era.</ref> This veneration became widely known as Tenjin Faith.
[[Image:Gagyu(sumatsunasiki tenmangu).JPG|thumb|right|270px|The reclining ox at Suma Tsunashiki Tenmangu, Kobe City]]
== History == Sugawara no Michizane was demoted and exiled to {{ill|Dazaifu|ja|大宰府}} due to the conspiracy of {{ill|Fujiwara no Tokihira|ja|藤原時平}}, dying there in despair.
After his death, Michizane’s vassal {{ill|Aji Sake Yasuyuki|ja|味酒安行}} enshrined him as the deity '''{{ill|Tenjin (kami)|lt=Tenman Daijizai Tenjin|ja|天満大自在天神}}'''.
Subsequently, an outbreak of plagues, droughts, and the deaths of {{ill|Emperor Daigo|ja|醍醐天皇}}'s princes caused fear of Michizane’s curse. The {{ill|Seiryōden lightning strike incident|ja|清涼殿落雷事件}} further linked Michizane’s spirit with the thunder god. The court pardoned Michizane’s alleged crimes and posthumously elevated his rank. In Kyoto’s {{ill|Kitano|ja|北野 (京都市)}}, Karatanojin, a local {{ill|landlord deity|ja|地主神}}, was enshrined as part of {{ill|Kitano Tenmangū|ja|北野天満宮}} to appease Michizane’s spirit.<ref>Karatanojin is now enshrined at the Hi no Miko Shrine within Kitano Tenmangū.</ref> Similarly, in Dazaifu, Michizane was worshipped at a mausoleum later developed into {{ill|Dazaifu Tenmangū|ja|太宰府天満宮}} by order of Emperor Daigo and [[:en:Fujiwara_no_Nakahira|Fujiwara no Nakahira]]. Additionally, {{ill|Osaka Tenmangū|ja|大阪天満宮}} was built in 949 after seven pine trees mysteriously sprouted overnight at a site believed to be under Michizane’s protection.
During the late Heian and Kamakura periods, the belief in Michizane’s wrath waned. According to the ''Tenjin Engi'' (天神縁起), he became revered as a merciful deity of integrity, poetry, and the arts, granting longevity in this life and bliss in the next.<ref>As Michizane was identified with the Eleven-Faced Kannon Bosatsu.</ref> Over time, Tenjin also became a protector against maritime disasters and was worshiped by royalty, military leaders, and merchants as a deity for victory and national protection. In the Edo period, Michizane was popularly venerated as the god of learning due to his scholarly achievements.
== Origin == {{ill|Kitano Tenmangū|ja|北野天満宮}} and {{ill|Dazaifu Tenmangū|ja|太宰府天満宮}} were established independently, each claiming to be the birthplace of Tenjin Faith. Others, such as {{ill|Hōfu Tenmangū|ja|防府天満宮}} and {{ill|Yoki Tenman Shrine|ja|與喜天満神社}}, also assert being the oldest origin sites.
== Local Tenjin Faith == [[Image:MATSUYAMA_CASTLE(IYO),TENJIN_YAGURA.JPG|thumb|right|250px|The Tenjin Yagura at Matsuyama Castle, Iyo Province]] [[Image:Japanese crest Kaga Umebachi.svg|thumb|right|150px|The Kaga Umebachi crest of the Maeda clan]]
; Hokuriku In {{ill|Fukui Prefecture|ja|福井県}} and {{ill|Toyama Prefecture|ja|富山県}}, Tenjin images are displayed in the alcove when a firstborn son is born. The tradition of offering flounder on January 25 is also observed. These practices were promoted by [[Matsudaira Yoshinaga]] during the late Edo period.
== Tenjin and the Number 25 == Michizane’s birth and death anniversaries fall on the 25th of June and February, respectively, leading to monthly festivals on the 25th. This association persists even today with events like the {{ill|Hajime Tenjin|ja|始め天神}} on January 25 and the {{ill|Shimai Tenjin|ja|終い天神}} on December 25.
== Tenjin and Oxen == Numerous legends link Michizane with oxen, portraying them as his loyal protectors and companions. Due to these stories, oxen are considered messengers of the deity and are often depicted in Tenmangū shrines as reclining ox statues.

'''Tenjin Faith''' (天神信仰) refers to the {{ill|faith|ja|信仰}} in Tenjin, the {{ill|Raijin|ja|雷神}} (god of thunder) in {{ill|Japan|ja|日本}}. Specifically, it pertains to the {{ill|Shinto|ja|神道}} belief system venerating {{ill|Sugawara no Michizane|ja|菅原道真}} as "Tenjin-sama," revered as a deity for fear and prayer.
Originally, Tenjin referred to {{ill|Amatsukami|ja|天津神}} as opposed to {{ill|Kunitsukami|ja|国津神}}, and it was not the name of a specific deity. However, shortly after Michizane’s death, he was enshrined as the deity '''{{ill|Tenjin (kami)|lt=Tenman Daijizai Tenjin|ja|天満大自在天神}}''' (てんまんだいじざいてんじん). Following the {{ill|Seiryōden lightning strike incident|ja|清涼殿落雷事件}}, Michizane’s vengeful spirit was associated with the local {{ill|Yakusanoikazuchi|ja|火雷大神}}, leading to his recognition as '''Karatentenjin''' (火雷天神). Later, Karatanojin was incorporated as a retinue spirit under titles like '''Nihon Daijō Itokuten''' (日本太政威徳天 / にほんだじょういとくてん) and even '''Jitsudō Gongen''' (実道権現)<ref>Cited in the Tenjinkyō (天神経), transmitted through terakoya (寺子屋).</ref>. Michizane’s spirit was further venerated in Buddhist texts such as ''Watokō Tenjin'' (渡唐天神), ''Myōhō Tenjinkyō'' (妙法天神経), and ''Tenjinkyō'' (天神経).<ref>Initially, Tenjin faith was closely tied to Buddhism, as monks from both the Tendai and Shingon schools were involved in its development. Temples and pagodas were often built on shrine grounds until the {{ill|Haibutsu Kishaku|ja|廃仏毀釈}} movement during the Meiji era.</ref> This veneration became widely known as Tenjin Faith.

According to the ''Tenjin Engi'' (天神縁起), he became revered as a merciful deity of integrity, poetry, and the arts, granting longevity in this life and bliss in the next.<ref>As Michizane was identified with the Eleven-Faced Kannon Bosatsu.</ref> Over time, Tenjin also became a protector against maritime disasters and was worshiped by royalty, military leaders, and merchants as a deity for victory and national protection. In the Edo period, Michizane was popularly venerated as the god of learning due to his scholarly achievements.
== Origin == {{ill|Kitano Tenmangū|ja|北野天満宮}} and {{ill|Dazaifu Tenmangū|ja|太宰府天満宮}} were established independently, each claiming to be the birthplace of Tenjin Faith. Others, such as {{ill|Hōfu Tenmangū|ja|防府天満宮}} and {{ill|Yoki Tenman Shrine|ja|與喜天満神社}}, also assert being the oldest origin sites.
== Local Tenjin Faith == [[Image:MATSUYAMA_CASTLE(IYO),TENJIN_YAGURA.JPG|thumb|right|250px|The Tenjin Yagura at Matsuyama Castle, Iyo Province]] [[Image:Japanese crest Kaga Umebachi.svg|thumb|right|150px|The Kaga Umebachi crest of the Maeda clan]]
; Hokuriku In {{ill|Fukui Prefecture|ja|福井県}} and {{ill|Toyama Prefecture|ja|富山県}}, Tenjin images are displayed in the alcove when a firstborn son is born. The tradition of offering flounder on January 25 is also observed. These practices were promoted by [[Matsudaira Yoshinaga]] during the late Edo period.
== Tenjin and the Number 25 == Michizane’s birth and death anniversaries fall on the 25th of June and February, respectively, leading to monthly festivals on the 25th. This association persists even today with events like the {{ill|Hajime Tenjin|ja|始め天神}} on January 25 and the {{ill|Shimai Tenjin|ja|終い天神}} on December 25.
== Tenjin and Oxen == Numerous legends link Michizane with oxen, portraying them as his loyal protectors and companions. Due to these stories, oxen are considered messengers of the deity and are often depicted in Tenmangū shrines as reclining ox statues.

[[ja:%E5%A4%A9%E7%A5%9E%E4%BF%A1%E4%BB%B0]] [[Category:Tenjin faith]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:qq-Tenjin faith-qq]] [[Category:Tenjin faith]][[Category:Tenjin faith]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:qq-Tenjin faith-qq]] [[Category:Tenjin faith]][[Category:bump 1-20 (2)]] [[Category:Subst:NAMESPACE]] [[Category:q--q]]

'''Tenjin Faith''' (天神信仰) refers to the {{ill|faith|ja|信仰}} in Tenjin, the {{ill|Raijin|ja|雷神}} (god of thunder) in {{ill|Japan|ja|日本}}. Specifically, it pertains to the {{ill|Shinto|ja|神道}} belief system venerating {{ill|Sugawara no Michizane|ja|菅原道真}} as "Tenjin-sama," revered as a deity for fear and prayer.
Originally, Tenjin referred to {{ill|Amatsukami|ja|天津神}} as opposed to {{ill|Kunitsukami|ja|国津神}}, and it was not the name of a specific deity. However, shortly after Michizane’s death, he was enshrined as the deity '''{{ill|Tenjin (kami)|lt=Tenman Daijizai Tenjin|ja|天満大自在天神}}''' (てんまんだいじざいてんじん). Following the {{ill|Seiryōden lightning strike incident|ja|清涼殿落雷事件}}, Michizane’s vengeful spirit was associated with the local {{ill|Yakusanoikazuchi|ja|火雷大神}}, leading to his recognition as '''Karatentenjin''' (火雷天神). Later, Karatanojin was incorporated as a retinue spirit under titles like '''Nihon Daijō Itokuten''' (日本太政威徳天 / にほんだじょういとくてん) and even '''Jitsudō Gongen''' (実道権現)<ref>Cited in the Tenjinkyō (天神経), transmitted through terakoya (寺子屋).</ref>. Michizane’s spirit was further venerated in Buddhist texts such as ''Watokō Tenjin'' (渡唐天神), ''Myōhō Tenjinkyō'' (妙法天神経), and ''Tenjinkyō'' (天神経).<ref>Initially, Tenjin faith was closely tied to Buddhism, as monks from both the Tendai and Shingon schools were involved in its development. Temples and pagodas were often built on shrine grounds until the {{ill|Haibutsu Kishaku|ja|廃仏毀釈}} movement during the Meiji era.</ref> This veneration became widely known as Tenjin Faith.
[[Image:Gagyu(sumatsunasiki tenmangu).JPG|thumb|right|270px|The reclining ox at Suma Tsunashiki Tenmangu, Kobe City]]
== History == Sugawara no Michizane was demoted and exiled to {{ill|Dazaifu|ja|大宰府}} due to the conspiracy of {{ill|Fujiwara no Tokihira|ja|藤原時平}}, dying there in despair.
After his death, Michizane’s vassal {{ill|Aji Sake Yasuyuki|ja|味酒安行}} enshrined him as the deity '''{{ill|Tenjin (kami)|lt=Tenman Daijizai Tenjin|ja|天満大自在天神}}'''.
Subsequently, an outbreak of plagues, droughts, and the deaths of {{ill|Emperor Daigo|ja|醍醐天皇}}'s princes caused fear of Michizane’s curse. The {{ill|Seiryōden lightning strike incident|ja|清涼殿落雷事件}} further linked Michizane’s spirit with the thunder god. The court pardoned Michizane’s alleged crimes and posthumously elevated his rank. In Kyoto’s {{ill|Kitano|ja|北野 (京都市)}}, Karatanojin, a local {{ill|landlord deity|ja|地主神}}, was enshrined as part of {{ill|Kitano Tenmangū|ja|北野天満宮}} to appease Michizane’s spirit.<ref>Karatanojin is now enshrined at the Hi no Miko Shrine within Kitano Tenmangū.</ref> Similarly, in Dazaifu, Michizane was worshipped at a mausoleum later developed into {{ill|Dazaifu Tenmangū|ja|太宰府天満宮}} by order of Emperor Daigo and [[:en:Fujiwara_no_Nakahira|Fujiwara no Nakahira]]. Additionally, {{ill|Osaka Tenmangū|ja|大阪天満宮}} was built in 949 after seven pine trees mysteriously sprouted overnight at a site believed to be under Michizane’s protection.
During the late Heian and Kamakura periods, the belief in Michizane’s wrath waned. According to the ''Tenjin Engi'' (天神縁起), he became revered as a merciful deity of integrity, poetry, and the arts, granting longevity in this life and bliss in the next.<ref>As Michizane was identified with the Eleven-Faced Kannon Bosatsu.</ref> Over time, Tenjin also became a protector against maritime disasters and was worshiped by royalty, military leaders, and merchants as a deity for victory and national protection. In the Edo period, Michizane was popularly venerated as the god of learning due to his scholarly achievements.
== Origin == {{ill|Kitano Tenmangū|ja|北野天満宮}} and {{ill|Dazaifu Tenmangū|ja|太宰府天満宮}} were established independently, each claiming to be the birthplace of Tenjin Faith. Others, such as {{ill|Hōfu Tenmangū|ja|防府天満宮}} and {{ill|Yoki Tenman Shrine|ja|與喜天満神社}}, also assert being the oldest origin sites.
== Local Tenjin Faith == [[Image:MATSUYAMA_CASTLE(IYO),TENJIN_YAGURA.JPG|thumb|right|250px|The Tenjin Yagura at Matsuyama Castle, Iyo Province]] [[Image:Japanese crest Kaga Umebachi.svg|thumb|right|150px|The Kaga Umebachi crest of the Maeda clan]]
; Hokuriku In {{ill|Fukui Prefecture|ja|福井県}} and {{ill|Toyama Prefecture|ja|富山県}}, Tenjin images are displayed in the alcove when a firstborn son is born. The tradition of offering flounder on January 25 is also observed. These practices were promoted by [[Matsudaira Yoshinaga]] during the late Edo period.
== Tenjin and the Number 25 == Michizane’s birth and death anniversaries fall on the 25th of June and February, respectively, leading to monthly festivals on the 25th. This association persists even today with events like the {{ill|Hajime Tenjin|ja|始め天神}} on January 25 and the {{ill|Shimai Tenjin|ja|終い天神}} on December 25.
== Tenjin and Oxen == Numerous legends link Michizane with oxen, portraying them as his loyal protectors and companions. Due to these stories, oxen are considered messengers of the deity and are often depicted in Tenmangū shrines as reclining ox statues.

'''Tenjin Faith''' (天神信仰) refers to the {{ill|faith|ja|信仰}} in Tenjin, the {{ill|Raijin|ja|雷神}} (god of thunder) in {{ill|Japan|ja|日本}}. Specifically, it pertains to the {{ill|Shinto|ja|神道}} belief system venerating {{ill|Sugawara no Michizane|ja|菅原道真}} as "Tenjin-sama," revered as a deity for fear and prayer.
Originally, Tenjin referred to {{ill|Amatsukami|ja|天津神}} as opposed to {{ill|Kunitsukami|ja|国津神}}, and it was not the name of a specific deity. However, shortly after Michizane’s death, he was enshrined as the deity '''{{ill|Tenjin (kami)|lt=Tenman Daijizai Tenjin|ja|天満大自在天神}}''' (てんまんだいじざいてんじん). Following the {{ill|Seiryōden lightning strike incident|ja|清涼殿落雷事件}}, Michizane’s vengeful spirit was associated with the local {{ill|Yakusanoikazuchi|ja|火雷大神}}, leading to his recognition as '''Karatentenjin''' (火雷天神). Later, Karatanojin was incorporated as a retinue spirit under titles like '''Nihon Daijō Itokuten''' (日本太政威徳天 / にほんだじょういとくてん) and even '''Jitsudō Gongen''' (実道権現)<ref>Cited in the Tenjinkyō (天神経), transmitted through terakoya (寺子屋).</ref>. Michizane’s spirit was further venerated in Buddhist texts such as ''Watokō Tenjin'' (渡唐天神), ''Myōhō Tenjinkyō'' (妙法天神経), and ''Tenjinkyō'' (天神経).<ref>Initially, Tenjin faith was closely tied to Buddhism, as monks from both the Tendai and Shingon schools were involved in its development. Temples and pagodas were often built on shrine grounds until the {{ill|Haibutsu Kishaku|ja|廃仏毀釈}} movement during the Meiji era.</ref> This veneration became widely known as Tenjin Faith.
[[Image:Gagyu(sumatsunasiki tenmangu).JPG|thumb|right|270px|The reclining ox at Suma Tsunashiki Tenmangu, Kobe City]]
== History == Sugawara no Michizane was demoted and exiled to {{ill|Dazaifu|ja|大宰府}} due to the conspiracy of {{ill|Fujiwara no Tokihira|ja|藤原時平}}, dying there in despair.
After his death, Michizane’s vassal {{ill|Aji Sake Yasuyuki|ja|味酒安行}} enshrined him as the deity '''{{ill|Tenjin (kami)|lt=Tenman Daijizai Tenjin|ja|天満大自在天神}}'''.
Subsequently, an outbreak of plagues, droughts, and the deaths of {{ill|Emperor Daigo|ja|醍醐天皇}}'s princes caused fear of Michizane’s curse. The {{ill|Seiryōden lightning strike incident|ja|清涼殿落雷事件}} further linked Michizane’s spirit with the thunder god. The court pardoned Michizane’s alleged crimes and posthumously elevated his rank. In Kyoto’s {{ill|Kitano|ja|北野 (京都市)}}, Karatanojin, a local {{ill|landlord deity|ja|地主神}}, was enshrined as part of {{ill|Kitano Tenmangū|ja|北野天満宮}} to appease Michizane’s spirit.<ref>Karatanojin is now enshrined at the Hi no Miko Shrine within Kitano Tenmangū.</ref> Similarly, in Dazaifu, Michizane was worshipped at a mausoleum later developed into {{ill|Dazaifu Tenmangū|ja|太宰府天満宮}} by order of Emperor Daigo and [[:en:Fujiwara_no_Nakahira|Fujiwara no Nakahira]]. Additionally, {{ill|Osaka Tenmangū|ja|大阪天満宮}} was built in 949 after seven pine trees mysteriously sprouted overnight at a site believed to be under Michizane’s protection.
During the late Heian and Kamakura periods, the belief in Michizane’s wrath waned. According to the ''Tenjin Engi'' (天神縁起), he became revered as a merciful deity of integrity, poetry, and the arts, granting longevity in this life and bliss in the next.<ref>As Michizane was identified with the Eleven-Faced Kannon Bosatsu.</ref> Over time, Tenjin also became a protector against maritime disasters and was worshiped by royalty, military leaders, and merchants as a deity for victory and national protection. In the Edo period, Michizane was popularly venerated as the god of learning due to his scholarly achievements.
== Origin == {{ill|Kitano Tenmangū|ja|北野天満宮}} and {{ill|Dazaifu Tenmangū|ja|太宰府天満宮}} were established independently, each claiming to be the birthplace of Tenjin Faith. Others, such as {{ill|Hōfu Tenmangū|ja|防府天満宮}} and {{ill|Yoki Tenman Shrine|ja|與喜天満神社}}, also assert being the oldest origin sites.
== Local Tenjin Faith == [[Image:MATSUYAMA_CASTLE(IYO),TENJIN_YAGURA.JPG|thumb|right|250px|The Tenjin Yagura at Matsuyama Castle, Iyo Province]] [[Image:Japanese crest Kaga Umebachi.svg|thumb|right|150px|The Kaga Umebachi crest of the Maeda clan]]
; Hokuriku In {{ill|Fukui Prefecture|ja|福井県}} and {{ill|Toyama Prefecture|ja|富山県}}, Tenjin images are displayed in the alcove when a firstborn son is born. The tradition of offering flounder on January 25 is also observed. These practices were promoted by [[Matsudaira Yoshinaga]] during the late Edo period.
== Tenjin and the Number 25 == Michizane’s birth and death anniversaries fall on the 25th of June and February, respectively, leading to monthly festivals on the 25th. This association persists even today with events like the {{ill|Hajime Tenjin|ja|始め天神}} on January 25 and the {{ill|Shimai Tenjin|ja|終い天神}} on December 25.
== Tenjin and Oxen == Numerous legends link Michizane with oxen, portraying them as his loyal protectors and companions. Due to these stories, oxen are considered messengers of the deity and are often depicted in Tenmangū shrines as reclining ox statues.

'''Tenjin Faith''' (天神信仰) refers to the {{ill|faith|ja|信仰}} in Tenjin, the {{ill|Raijin|ja|雷神}} (god of thunder) in {{ill|Japan|ja|日本}}. Specifically, it pertains to the {{ill|Shinto|ja|神道}} belief system venerating {{ill|Sugawara no Michizane|ja|菅原道真}} as "Tenjin-sama," revered as a deity for fear and prayer.
Originally, Tenjin referred to {{ill|Amatsukami|ja|天津神}} as opposed to {{ill|Kunitsukami|ja|国津神}}, and it was not the name of a specific deity. However, shortly after Michizane’s death, he was enshrined as the deity '''{{ill|Tenjin (kami)|lt=Tenman Daijizai Tenjin|ja|天満大自在天神}}''' (てんまんだいじざいてんじん). Following the {{ill|Seiryōden lightning strike incident|ja|清涼殿落雷事件}}, Michizane’s vengeful spirit was associated with the local {{ill|Yakusanoikazuchi|ja|火雷大神}}, leading to his recognition as '''Karatentenjin''' (火雷天神). Later, Karatanojin was incorporated as a retinue spirit under titles like '''Nihon Daijō Itokuten''' (日本太政威徳天 / にほんだじょういとくてん) and even '''Jitsudō Gongen''' (実道権現)<ref>Cited in the Tenjinkyō (天神経), transmitted through terakoya (寺子屋).</ref>. Michizane’s spirit was further venerated in Buddhist texts such as ''Watokō Tenjin'' (渡唐天神), ''Myōhō Tenjinkyō'' (妙法天神経), and ''Tenjinkyō'' (天神経).<ref>Initially, Tenjin faith was closely tied to Buddhism, as monks from both the Tendai and Shingon schools were involved in its development. Temples and pagodas were often built on shrine grounds until the {{ill|Haibutsu Kishaku|ja|廃仏毀釈}} movement during the Meiji era.</ref> This veneration became widely known as Tenjin Faith.
[[Image:Gagyu(sumatsunasiki tenmangu).JPG|thumb|right|270px|The reclining ox at Suma Tsunashiki Tenmangu, Kobe City]]
== History == Sugawara no Michizane was demoted and exiled to {{ill|Dazaifu|ja|大宰府}} due to the conspiracy of {{ill|Fujiwara no Tokihira|ja|藤原時平}}, dying there in despair.
After his death, Michizane’s vassal {{ill|Aji Sake Yasuyuki|ja|味酒安行}} enshrined him as the deity '''{{ill|Tenjin (kami)|lt=Tenman Daijizai Tenjin|ja|天満大自在天神}}'''.
Subsequently, an outbreak of plagues, droughts, and the deaths of {{ill|Emperor Daigo|ja|醍醐天皇}}'s princes caused fear of Michizane’s curse. The {{ill|Seiryōden lightning strike incident|ja|清涼殿落雷事件}} further linked Michizane’s spirit with the thunder god. The court pardoned Michizane’s alleged crimes and posthumously elevated his rank. In Kyoto’s {{ill|Kitano|ja|北野 (京都市)}}, Karatanojin, a local {{ill|landlord deity|ja|地主神}}, was enshrined as part of {{ill|Kitano Tenmangū|ja|北野天満宮}} to appease Michizane’s spirit.<ref>Karatanojin is now enshrined at the Hi no Miko Shrine within Kitano Tenmangū.</ref> Similarly, in Dazaifu, Michizane was worshipped at a mausoleum later developed into {{ill|Dazaifu Tenmangū|ja|太宰府天満宮}} by order of Emperor Daigo and [[:en:Fujiwara_no_Nakahira|Fujiwara no Nakahira]]. Additionally, {{ill|Osaka Tenmangū|ja|大阪天満宮}} was built in 949 after seven pine trees mysteriously sprouted overnight at a site believed to be under Michizane’s protection.
During the late Heian and Kamakura periods, the belief in Michizane’s wrath waned. According to the ''Tenjin Engi'' (天神縁起), he became revered as a merciful deity of integrity, poetry, and the arts, granting longevity in this life and bliss in the next.<ref>As Michizane was identified with the Eleven-Faced Kannon Bosatsu.</ref> Over time, Tenjin also became a protector against maritime disasters and was worshiped by royalty, military leaders, and merchants as a deity for victory and national protection. In the Edo period, Michizane was popularly venerated as the god of learning due to his scholarly achievements.
== Origin == {{ill|Kitano Tenmangū|ja|北野天満宮}} and {{ill|Dazaifu Tenmangū|ja|太宰府天満宮}} were established independently, each claiming to be the birthplace of Tenjin Faith. Others, such as {{ill|Hōfu Tenmangū|ja|防府天満宮}} and {{ill|Yoki Tenman Shrine|ja|與喜天満神社}}, also assert being the oldest origin sites.
== Local Tenjin Faith == [[Image:MATSUYAMA_CASTLE(IYO),TENJIN_YAGURA.JPG|thumb|right|250px|The Tenjin Yagura at Matsuyama Castle, Iyo Province]] [[Image:Japanese crest Kaga Umebachi.svg|thumb|right|150px|The Kaga Umebachi crest of the Maeda clan]]
; Hokuriku In {{ill|Fukui Prefecture|ja|福井県}} and {{ill|Toyama Prefecture|ja|富山県}}, Tenjin images are displayed in the alcove when a firstborn son is born. The tradition of offering flounder on January 25 is also observed. These practices were promoted by [[Matsudaira Yoshinaga]] during the late Edo period.
== Tenjin and the Number 25 == Michizane’s birth and death anniversaries fall on the 25th of June and February, respectively, leading to monthly festivals on the 25th. This association persists even today with events like the {{ill|Hajime Tenjin|ja|始め天神}} on January 25 and the {{ill|Shimai Tenjin|ja|終い天神}} on December 25.
== Tenjin and Oxen == Numerous legends link Michizane with oxen, portraying them as his loyal protectors and companions. Due to these stories, oxen are considered messengers of the deity and are often depicted in Tenmangū shrines as reclining ox statues.

'''Tenjin Faith''' (天神信仰) refers to the {{ill|faith|ja|信仰}} in Tenjin, the {{ill|Raijin|ja|雷神}} (god of thunder) in {{ill|Japan|ja|日本}}. Specifically, it pertains to the {{ill|Shinto|ja|神道}} belief system venerating {{ill|Sugawara no Michizane|ja|菅原道真}} as "Tenjin-sama," revered as a deity for fear and prayer.
Originally, Tenjin referred to {{ill|Amatsukami|ja|天津神}} as opposed to {{ill|Kunitsukami|ja|国津神}}, and it was not the name of a specific deity. However, shortly after Michizane’s death, he was enshrined as the deity '''{{ill|Tenjin (kami)|lt=Tenman Daijizai Tenjin|ja|天満大自在天神}}''' (てんまんだいじざいてんじん). Following the {{ill|Seiryōden lightning strike incident|ja|清涼殿落雷事件}}, Michizane’s vengeful spirit was associated with the local {{ill|Yakusanoikazuchi|ja|火雷大神}}, leading to his recognition as '''Karatentenjin''' (火雷天神). Later, Karatanojin was incorporated as a retinue spirit under titles like '''Nihon Daijō Itokuten''' (日本太政威徳天 / にほんだじょういとくてん) and even '''Jitsudō Gongen''' (実道権現)<ref>Cited in the Tenjinkyō (天神経), transmitted through terakoya (寺子屋).</ref>. Michizane’s spirit was further venerated in Buddhist texts such as ''Watokō Tenjin'' (渡唐天神), ''Myōhō Tenjinkyō'' (妙法天神経), and ''Tenjinkyō'' (天神経).<ref>Initially, Tenjin faith was closely tied to Buddhism, as monks from both the Tendai and Shingon schools were involved in its development. Temples and pagodas were often built on shrine grounds until the {{ill|Haibutsu Kishaku|ja|廃仏毀釈}} movement during the Meiji era.</ref> This veneration became widely known as Tenjin Faith.
[[Image:Gagyu(sumatsunasiki tenmangu).JPG|thumb|right|270px|The reclining ox at Suma Tsunashiki Tenmangu, Kobe City]]
== History == Sugawara no Michizane was demoted and exiled to {{ill|Dazaifu|ja|大宰府}} due to the conspiracy of {{ill|Fujiwara no Tokihira|ja|藤原時平}}, dying there in despair.
After his death, Michizane’s vassal {{ill|Aji Sake Yasuyuki|ja|味酒安行}} enshrined him as the deity '''{{ill|Tenjin (kami)|lt=Tenman Daijizai Tenjin|ja|天満大自在天神}}'''.
Subsequently, an outbreak of plagues, droughts, and the deaths of {{ill|Emperor Daigo|ja|醍醐天皇}}'s princes caused fear of Michizane’s curse. The {{ill|Seiryōden lightning strike incident|ja|清涼殿落雷事件}} further linked Michizane’s spirit with the thunder god. The court pardoned Michizane’s alleged crimes and posthumously elevated his rank. In Kyoto’s {{ill|Kitano|ja|北野 (京都市)}}, Karatanojin, a local {{ill|landlord deity|ja|地主神}}, was enshrined as part of {{ill|Kitano Tenmangū|ja|北野天満宮}} to appease Michizane’s spirit.<ref>Karatanojin is now enshrined at the Hi no Miko Shrine within Kitano Tenmangū.</ref> Similarly, in Dazaifu, Michizane was worshipped at a mausoleum later developed into {{ill|Dazaifu Tenmangū|ja|太宰府天満宮}} by order of Emperor Daigo and [[:en:Fujiwara_no_Nakahira|Fujiwara no Nakahira]]. Additionally, {{ill|Osaka Tenmangū|ja|大阪天満宮}} was built in 949 after seven pine trees mysteriously sprouted overnight at a site believed to be under Michizane’s protection.
During the late Heian and Kamakura periods, the belief in Michizane’s wrath waned. According to the ''Tenjin Engi'' (天神縁起), he became revered as a merciful deity of integrity, poetry, and the arts, granting longevity in this life and bliss in the next.<ref>As Michizane was identified with the Eleven-Faced Kannon Bosatsu.</ref> Over time, Tenjin also became a protector against maritime disasters and was worshiped by royalty, military leaders, and merchants as a deity for victory and national protection. In the Edo period, Michizane was popularly venerated as the god of learning due to his scholarly achievements.
== Origin == {{ill|Kitano Tenmangū|ja|北野天満宮}} and {{ill|Dazaifu Tenmangū|ja|太宰府天満宮}} were established independently, each claiming to be the birthplace of Tenjin Faith. Others, such as {{ill|Hōfu Tenmangū|ja|防府天満宮}} and {{ill|Yoki Tenman Shrine|ja|與喜天満神社}}, also assert being the oldest origin sites.
== Local Tenjin Faith == [[Image:MATSUYAMA_CASTLE(IYO),TENJIN_YAGURA.JPG|thumb|right|250px|The Tenjin Yagura at Matsuyama Castle, Iyo Province]] [[Image:Japanese crest Kaga Umebachi.svg|thumb|right|150px|The Kaga Umebachi crest of the Maeda clan]]
; Hokuriku In {{ill|Fukui Prefecture|ja|福井県}} and {{ill|Toyama Prefecture|ja|富山県}}, Tenjin images are displayed in the alcove when a firstborn son is born. The tradition of offering flounder on January 25 is also observed. These practices were promoted by [[Matsudaira Yoshinaga]] during the late Edo period.
== Tenjin and the Number 25 == Michizane’s birth and death anniversaries fall on the 25th of June and February, respectively, leading to monthly festivals on the 25th. This association persists even today with events like the {{ill|Hajime Tenjin|ja|始め天神}} on January 25 and the {{ill|Shimai Tenjin|ja|終い天神}} on December 25.
== Tenjin and Oxen == Numerous legends link Michizane with oxen, portraying them as his loyal protectors and companions. Due to these stories, oxen are considered messengers of the deity and are often depicted in Tenmangū shrines as reclining ox statues.

[[ja:%E5%A4%A9%E7%A5%9E%E4%BF%A1%E4%BB%B0]] [[Category:Tenjin faith]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:qq-Tenjin faith-qq]] [[Category:Tenjin faith]][[Category:Tenjin faith]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:qq-Tenjin faith-qq]] [[Category:Tenjin faith]][[Category:bump 1-20 (2)]] [[Category:q--q]] [[Category:q--q]] [[sn:Tenjin faith]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Tenjin faith]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Tenjin faith]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Tenjin faith]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Tenjin faith]][[Category:qq]] [[sn:Tenjin faith]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Tenjin faith]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Tenjin faith]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Tenjin faith]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Tenjin faith]][[Category:qq]] [[sn:Tenjin faith]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Tenjin faith]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Tenjin faith]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Tenjin faith]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:subst:NAMESPACE]][[sn:Tenjin faith]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Tenjin faith]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Tenjin faith]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Tenjin faith]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Tenjin faith]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Tenjin faith]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:11]][[ja:Tenjin faith]][[Category:11]]

'''Tenjin Faith''' (天神信仰) refers to the {{ill|faith|ja|信仰}} in Tenjin, the {{ill|Raijin|ja|雷神}} (god of thunder) in {{ill|Japan|ja|日本}}. Specifically, it pertains to the {{ill|Shinto|ja|神道}} belief system venerating {{ill|Sugawara no Michizane|ja|菅原道真}} as "Tenjin-sama," revered as a deity for fear and prayer.
Originally, Tenjin referred to {{ill|Amatsukami|ja|天津神}} as opposed to {{ill|Kunitsukami|ja|国津神}}, and it was not the name of a specific deity. However, shortly after Michizane’s death, he was enshrined as the deity '''{{ill|Tenjin (kami)|lt=Tenman Daijizai Tenjin|ja|天満大自在天神}}''' (てんまんだいじざいてんじん). Following the {{ill|Seiryōden lightning strike incident|ja|清涼殿落雷事件}}, Michizane’s vengeful spirit was associated with the local {{ill|Yakusanoikazuchi|ja|火雷大神}}, leading to his recognition as '''Karatentenjin''' (火雷天神). Later, Karatanojin was incorporated as a retinue spirit under titles like '''Nihon Daijō Itokuten''' (日本太政威徳天 / にほんだじょういとくてん) and even '''Jitsudō Gongen''' (実道権現)<ref>Cited in the Tenjinkyō (天神経), transmitted through terakoya (寺子屋).</ref>. Michizane’s spirit was further venerated in Buddhist texts such as ''Watokō Tenjin'' (渡唐天神), ''Myōhō Tenjinkyō'' (妙法天神経), and ''Tenjinkyō'' (天神経).<ref>Initially, Tenjin faith was closely tied to Buddhism, as monks from both the Tendai and Shingon schools were involved in its development. Temples and pagodas were often built on shrine grounds until the {{ill|Haibutsu Kishaku|ja|廃仏毀釈}} movement during the Meiji era.</ref> This veneration became widely known as Tenjin Faith.
[[Image:Gagyu(sumatsunasiki tenmangu).JPG|thumb|right|270px|The reclining ox at Suma Tsunashiki Tenmangu, Kobe City]]
== History == Sugawara no Michizane was demoted and exiled to {{ill|Dazaifu|ja|大宰府}} due to the conspiracy of {{ill|Fujiwara no Tokihira|ja|藤原時平}}, dying there in despair.
After his death, Michizane’s vassal {{ill|Aji Sake Yasuyuki|ja|味酒安行}} enshrined him as the deity '''{{ill|Tenjin (kami)|lt=Tenman Daijizai Tenjin|ja|天満大自在天神}}'''.
Subsequently, an outbreak of plagues, droughts, and the deaths of {{ill|Emperor Daigo|ja|醍醐天皇}}'s princes caused fear of Michizane’s curse. The {{ill|Seiryōden lightning strike incident|ja|清涼殿落雷事件}} further linked Michizane’s spirit with the thunder god. The court pardoned Michizane’s alleged crimes and posthumously elevated his rank. In Kyoto’s {{ill|Kitano|ja|北野 (京都市)}}, Karatanojin, a local {{ill|landlord deity|ja|地主神}}, was enshrined as part of {{ill|Kitano Tenmangū|ja|北野天満宮}} to appease Michizane’s spirit.<ref>Karatanojin is now enshrined at the Hi no Miko Shrine within Kitano Tenmangū.</ref> Similarly, in Dazaifu, Michizane was worshipped at a mausoleum later developed into {{ill|Dazaifu Tenmangū|ja|太宰府天満宮}} by order of Emperor Daigo and [[:en:Fujiwara_no_Nakahira|Fujiwara no Nakahira]]. Additionally, {{ill|Osaka Tenmangū|ja|大阪天満宮}} was built in 949 after seven pine trees mysteriously sprouted overnight at a site believed to be under Michizane’s protection.
During the late Heian and Kamakura periods, the belief in Michizane’s wrath waned. According to the ''Tenjin Engi'' (天神縁起), he became revered as a merciful deity of integrity, poetry, and the arts, granting longevity in this life and bliss in the next.<ref>As Michizane was identified with the Eleven-Faced Kannon Bosatsu.</ref> Over time, Tenjin also became a protector against maritime disasters and was worshiped by royalty, military leaders, and merchants as a deity for victory and national protection. In the Edo period, Michizane was popularly venerated as the god of learning due to his scholarly achievements.
== Origin == {{ill|Kitano Tenmangū|ja|北野天満宮}} and {{ill|Dazaifu Tenmangū|ja|太宰府天満宮}} were established independently, each claiming to be the birthplace of Tenjin Faith. Others, such as {{ill|Hōfu Tenmangū|ja|防府天満宮}} and {{ill|Yoki Tenman Shrine|ja|與喜天満神社}}, also assert being the oldest origin sites.
== Local Tenjin Faith == [[Image:MATSUYAMA_CASTLE(IYO),TENJIN_YAGURA.JPG|thumb|right|250px|The Tenjin Yagura at Matsuyama Castle, Iyo Province]] [[Image:Japanese crest Kaga Umebachi.svg|thumb|right|150px|The Kaga Umebachi crest of the Maeda clan]]
; Hokuriku In {{ill|Fukui Prefecture|ja|福井県}} and {{ill|Toyama Prefecture|ja|富山県}}, Tenjin images are displayed in the alcove when a firstborn son is born. The tradition of offering flounder on January 25 is also observed. These practices were promoted by [[Matsudaira Yoshinaga]] during the late Edo period.
== Tenjin and the Number 25 == Michizane’s birth and death anniversaries fall on the 25th of June and February, respectively, leading to monthly festivals on the 25th. This association persists even today with events like the {{ill|Hajime Tenjin|ja|始め天神}} on January 25 and the {{ill|Shimai Tenjin|ja|終い天神}} on December 25.
== Tenjin and Oxen == Numerous legends link Michizane with oxen, portraying them as his loyal protectors and companions. Due to these stories, oxen are considered messengers of the deity and are often depicted in Tenmangū shrines as reclining ox statues.
== References == {{Reflist|2}}

revision.comment: Text replacement - "鷽替え}}" to "鷽替え|1=Uso-kae Festival}}"
revision.text:
'''Tenjin Faith''' (天神信仰) refers to the {{ill|faith|ja|信仰}} in Tenjin, the {{ill|Raijin|ja|雷神}} (god of thunder) in {{ill|Japan|ja|日本}}. Specifically, it pertains to the {{ill|Shinto|ja|神道}} belief system venerating {{ill|Sugawara no Michizane|ja|菅原道真}} as "Tenjin-sama," revered as a deity for fear and prayer.
Originally, Tenjin referred to {{ill|Amatsukami|ja|天津神}} as opposed to {{ill|Kunitsukami|ja|国津神}}, and it was not the name of a specific deity. However, shortly after Michizane’s death, he was enshrined as the deity '''{{ill|Tenjin (kami)|lt=Tenman Daijizai Tenjin|ja|天満大自在天神}}''' (てんまんだいじざいてんじん). Following the {{ill|Seiryōden lightning strike incident|ja|清涼殿落雷事件}}, Michizane’s vengeful spirit was associated with the local {{ill|Yakusanoikazuchi|ja|火雷大神}}, leading to his recognition as '''Karatentenjin''' (火雷天神). Later, Karatanojin was incorporated as a retinue spirit under titles like '''Nihon Daijō Itokuten''' (日本太政威徳天 / にほんだじょういとくてん) and even '''Jitsudō Gongen''' (実道権現)<ref>Cited in the Tenjinkyō (天神経), transmitted through terakoya (寺子屋).</ref>. Michizane’s spirit was further venerated in Buddhist texts such as ''Watokō Tenjin'' (渡唐天神), ''Myōhō Tenjinkyō'' (妙法天神経), and ''Tenjinkyō'' (天神経).<ref>Initially, Tenjin faith was closely tied to Buddhism, as monks from both the Tendai and Shingon schools were involved in its development. Temples and pagodas were often built on shrine grounds until the {{ill|Haibutsu Kishaku|ja|廃仏毀釈}} movement during the Meiji era.</ref> This veneration became widely known as Tenjin Faith.
[[Image:Gagyu(sumatsunasiki tenmangu).JPG|thumb|right|270px|The reclining ox at Suma Tsunashiki Tenmangu, Kobe City]]
== History == Sugawara no Michizane was demoted and exiled to {{ill|Dazaifu|ja|大宰府}} due to the conspiracy of {{ill|Fujiwara no Tokihira|ja|藤原時平}}, dying there in despair.
After his death, Michizane’s vassal {{ill|Aji Sake Yasuyuki|ja|味酒安行}} enshrined him as the deity '''{{ill|Tenjin (kami)|lt=Tenman Daijizai Tenjin|ja|天満大自在天神}}'''.
Subsequently, an outbreak of plagues, droughts, and the deaths of {{ill|Emperor Daigo|ja|醍醐天皇}}'s princes caused fear of Michizane’s curse. The {{ill|Seiryōden lightning strike incident|ja|清涼殿落雷事件}} further linked Michizane’s spirit with the thunder god. The court pardoned Michizane’s alleged crimes and posthumously elevated his rank. In Kyoto’s {{ill|Kitano|ja|北野 (京都市)}}, Karatanojin, a local {{ill|landlord deity|ja|地主神}}, was enshrined as part of {{ill|Kitano Tenmangū|ja|北野天満宮}} to appease Michizane’s spirit.<ref>Karatanojin is now enshrined at the Hi no Miko Shrine within Kitano Tenmangū.</ref> Similarly, in Dazaifu, Michizane was worshipped at a mausoleum later developed into {{ill|Dazaifu Tenmangū|ja|太宰府天満宮}} by order of Emperor Daigo and [[:en:Fujiwara_no_Nakahira|Fujiwara no Nakahira]]. Additionally, {{ill|Osaka Tenmangū|ja|大阪天満宮}} was built in 949 after seven pine trees mysteriously sprouted overnight at a site believed to be under Michizane’s protection.
During the late Heian and Kamakura periods, the belief in Michizane’s wrath waned. According to the ''Tenjin Engi'' (天神縁起), he became revered as a merciful deity of integrity, poetry, and the arts, granting longevity in this life and bliss in the next.<ref>As Michizane was identified with the Eleven-Faced Kannon Bosatsu.</ref> Over time, Tenjin also became a protector against maritime disasters and was worshiped by royalty, military leaders, and merchants as a deity for victory and national protection. In the Edo period, Michizane was popularly venerated as the god of learning due to his scholarly achievements.
== Origin == {{ill|Kitano Tenmangū|ja|北野天満宮}} and {{ill|Dazaifu Tenmangū|ja|太宰府天満宮}} were established independently, each claiming to be the birthplace of Tenjin Faith. Others, such as {{ill|Hōfu Tenmangū|ja|防府天満宮}} and {{ill|Yoki Tenman Shrine|ja|與喜天満神社}}, also assert being the oldest origin sites.
== Local Tenjin Faith == [[Image:MATSUYAMA_CASTLE(IYO),TENJIN_YAGURA.JPG|thumb|right|250px|The Tenjin Yagura at Matsuyama Castle, Iyo Province]] [[Image:Japanese crest Kaga Umebachi.svg|thumb|right|150px|The Kaga Umebachi crest of the Maeda clan]]
; Hokuriku In {{ill|Fukui Prefecture|ja|福井県}} and {{ill|Toyama Prefecture|ja|富山県}}, Tenjin images are displayed in the alcove when a firstborn son is born. The tradition of offering flounder on January 25 is also observed. These practices were promoted by [[Matsudaira Yoshinaga]] during the late Edo period.
== Tenjin and the Number 25 == Michizane’s birth and death anniversaries fall on the 25th of June and February, respectively, leading to monthly festivals on the 25th. This association persists even today with events like the {{ill|Hajime Tenjin|ja|始め天神}} on January 25 and the {{ill|Shimai Tenjin|ja|終い天神}} on December 25.
== Tenjin and Oxen == Numerous legends link Michizane with oxen, portraying them as his loyal protectors and companions. Due to these stories, oxen are considered messengers of the deity and are often depicted in Tenmangū shrines as reclining ox statues.
== References == {{Reflist|2}}
== Related Articles == * {{ill|Tenjin (kami)|lt=Tenman Daijizai Tenjin|ja|天満大自在天神}} * {{ill|Tenmangū|ja|天満宮}} * {{ill|Tenjin Matsuri|ja|天神祭}} * {{ill|Flying Plum|ja|飛梅}} * {{ill|Umegae Mochi|ja|梅ヶ枝餅}} * {{ill|Uso-kae|ja|鷽替え|1=Uso-kae Festival}} * {{ill|Kitano Ōchanoyu|ja|北野大茶会}} * {{ill|Japanese-Chinese Synthesis|ja|和魂漢才}} * {{ill|Umebachi|ja|梅鉢}}
== External Links == {{Shinto Portal|[[Image:Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|40px]]}} * [http://www.jinjahoncho.or.jp/2007/02/01%E3%80%80%E5%A4%A9%E7%A5%9E%E3%81%95%E3%81%BE/ Tenjin-sama - Jinja Honchō]{{Dead link|date=August 2024}} * [http://www.tokyo-jinjacho.or.jp/syoukai/21_itabashi/21021.html Kitano Shrine, Tokumaru, Itabashi Ward, Tokyo]

== Tenjin and Oxen == Numerous legends link Michizane with oxen, portraying them as his loyal protectors and companions. Due to these stories, oxen are considered messengers of the deity and are often depicted in Tenmangū shrines as reclining ox statues.
