<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Tenjin cult]][[ja:天神信仰]][[ko:천신신앙]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q66890725]] Wikidata name: Tenjin faith [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:53, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Tenjin Faith''' (天神信仰) refers to the [[:en:Faith|faith]] in Tenjin, the [[:en:Raijin|Raijin]] (god of thunder) in [[:en:Japan|Japan]]. Specifically, it pertains to the [[:en:Shinto|Shinto]] belief system venerating {{ill|Sugawara no Michizane|1=Sugawara no Michizane|2=ja|3=菅原道真|ja|Sugawara no Michizane|1=Sugawara no Michizane|2=ja|3=菅原道真|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sugawara no Michizane|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q382005}} as "Tenjin-sama," revered as a deity for fear and prayer.
Originally, Tenjin referred to {{ill|Amatsukami|ja|天津神|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/matsukami|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 天津神・国津神|comment=confirming this goes to Amatsukami|1=Amatsukami|2=zh|3=天神 (日本神話)|4=fr|5=Amatsukami|6=mni|7=ꯑꯃꯠꯁꯨꯀꯃꯤ|comment=confirming this goes to Amatsukami|1=Amatsukami|2=zh|3=天神 (日本神話)|4=fr|5=Amatsukami|6=mni|7=ꯑꯃꯠꯁꯨꯀꯃꯤ}} as opposed to {{ill|Kunitsukami|ja|国津神|1=Kunitsukami|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kunitsukami|13=User:Immanuelle/Kunitsukami|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 天津神・国津神|comment=confirming this goes to Kunitsukami|1=Kunitsukami|2=zh|3=國神|4=fr|5=Kunitsukami|6=mni|7=ꯀꯨꯅꯤꯠꯁꯨꯀꯃꯤ}}, and it was not the name of a specific deity. However, shortly after Michizane’s death, he was enshrined as the deity '''[[:en:Tenjin (kami)|Tenman Daijizai Tenjin]]''' (てんまんだいじざいてんじん). Following the {{ill|Seiryōden lightning strike incident|ja|清涼殿落雷事件|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Seiryōden lightning strike incident|qq=}}, Michizane’s vengeful spirit was associated with the local [[:en:Yakusanoikazuchi|Yakusanoikazuchi]], leading to his recognition as '''Karatentenjin''' (火雷天神). Later, Karatanojin was incorporated as a retinue spirit under titles like '''Nihon Daijō Itokuten''' (日本太政威徳天 / にほんだじょういとくてん) and even '''Jitsudō Gongen''' (実道権現)<ref>Cited in the Tenjinkyō (天神経), transmitted through terakoya (寺子屋).</ref>. Michizane’s spirit was further venerated in Buddhist texts such as ''Watokō Tenjin'' (渡唐天神), ''Myōhō Tenjinkyō'' (妙法天神経), and ''Tenjinkyō'' (天神経).<ref>Initially, Tenjin faith was closely tied to Buddhism, as monks from both the Tendai and Shingon schools were involved in its development. Temples and pagodas were often built on shrine grounds until the [[:en:Haibutsu kishaku|Haibutsu kishaku]] movement during the Meiji era.</ref> This veneration became widely known as Tenjin Faith.
[[Image:Gagyu(sumatsunasiki tenmangu).JPG|thumb|right|270px|The reclining ox at Suma Tsunashiki Tenmangu, Kobe City]]
== History == Sugawara no Michizane was demoted and exiled to [[:en:Dazaifu (government)|Dazaifu]] due to the conspiracy of [[:en:Fujiwara no Tokihira|Fujiwara no Tokihira]], dying there in despair.
After his death, Michizane’s vassal {{ill|Aji Sake Yasuyuki|ja|味酒安行|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Aji Sake Yasuyuki|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} enshrined him as the deity '''[[:en:Tenjin (kami)|Tenman Daijizai Tenjin]]'''.
Subsequently, an outbreak of plagues, droughts, and the deaths of [[:en:Emperor Daigo|Emperor Daigo]]'s princes caused fear of Michizane’s curse. The {{ill|Seiryōden lightning strike incident|ja|清涼殿落雷事件|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Seiryōden lightning strike incident|qq=}} further linked Michizane’s spirit with the thunder god. The court pardoned Michizane’s alleged crimes and posthumously elevated his Rank. In Kyoto’s {{ill|Kitano|ja|北野 (京都市)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kitano|qq=|1=Kitano|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11404253}}, Karatanojin, a local [[:en:Landlord deity|landlord deity]], was enshrined as part of [[:en:Kitano Tenmangū|Kitano Tenmangū]] to appease Michizane’s spirit.<ref>Karatanojin is now enshrined at the Hi no Miko Shrine within Kitano Tenmangū.</ref> Similarly, in Dazaifu, Michizane was worshipped at a mausoleum later developed into [[:en:Dazaifu Tenmangū|Dazaifu Tenmangū]] by order of Emperor Daigo and [[:en:Fujiwara_no_Nakahira|Fujiwara no Nakahira]]. Additionally, [[:en:Osaka Tenmangū|Osaka Tenmangū]] was built in 949 after seven pine trees mysteriously sprouted overnight at a site believed to be under Michizane’s protection.
During the late Heian and Kamakura periods, the belief in Michizane’s wrath waned. According to the ''Tenjin Engi'' (天神縁起), he became revered as a merciful deity of integrity, poetry, and the arts, granting longevity in this life and bliss in the next.<ref>As Michizane was identified with the Eleven-Faced Kannon Bosatsu.</ref> Over time, Tenjin also became a protector against maritime disasters and was worshiped by royalty, military leaders, and merchants as a deity for victory and national protection. In the Edo period, Michizane was popularly venerated as the god of learning due to his scholarly achievements.
== Origin == [[:en:Kitano Tenmangū|Kitano Tenmangū]] and [[:en:Dazaifu Tenmangū|Dazaifu Tenmangū]] were established independently, each claiming to be the birthplace of Tenjin Faith. Others, such as [[:en:Hōfu Tenmangū|Hōfu Tenmangū]] and {{ill|Yoki Tenman Shrine|ja|與喜天満神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yoki Tenman Shrine|qq=}}, also assert being the oldest origin sites.
== Local Tenjin Faith == [[Image:MATSUYAMA_CASTLE(IYO),TENJIN_YAGURA.JPG|thumb|right|250px|The Tenjin Yagura at Matsuyama Castle, Iyo Province]] [[Image:Japanese crest Kaga Umebachi.svg|thumb|right|150px|The Kaga Umebachi crest of the Maeda clan]]
; Hokuriku In [[:en:Fukui Prefecture|Fukui Prefecture]] and [[:en:Toyama Prefecture|Toyama Prefecture]], Tenjin images are displayed in the alcove when a firstborn son is born. The tradition of offering flounder on January 25 is also observed. These practices were promoted by [[:en:Matsudaira Yoshinaga|Matsudaira Yoshinaga]] during the late Edo period.
== Tenjin and the Number 25 == Michizane’s birth and death anniversaries fall on the 25th of June and February, respectively, leading to monthly festivals on the 25th. This association persists even today with events like the {{ill|Hajime Tenjin|ja|始め天神|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hajime Tenjin|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} on January 25 and the {{ill|Shimai Tenjin|ja|終い天神|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shimai Tenjin|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} on December 25.
== Tenjin and Oxen == Numerous legends link Michizane with oxen, portraying them as his loyal protectors and companions. Due to these stories, oxen are considered messengers of the deity and are often depicted in Tenmangū shrines as reclining ox statues.

== History == Sugawara no Michizane was demoted and exiled to [[:en:Dazaifu (government)|Dazaifu]] due to the conspiracy of [[:en:Fujiwara no Tokihira|Fujiwara no Tokihira]], dying there in despair.
After his death, Michizane’s vassal {{ill|Aji Sake Yasuyuki|ja|味酒安行|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Aji Sake Yasuyuki|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} enshrined him as the deity '''[[:en:Tenjin (kami)|Tenman Daijizai Tenjin]]'''.
Subsequently, an outbreak of plagues, droughts, and the deaths of [[:en:Emperor Daigo|Emperor Daigo]]'s princes caused fear of Michizane’s curse. The {{ill|Seiryōden lightning strike incident|ja|清涼殿落雷事件|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Seiryōden lightning strike incident|qq=}} further linked Michizane’s spirit with the thunder god. The court pardoned Michizane’s alleged crimes and posthumously elevated his Rank. In Kyoto’s {{ill|Kitano|ja|北野 (京都市)|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kitano|1=Kitano|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11404253}}, Karatanojin, a local [[:en:Landlord deity|landlord deity]], was enshrined as part of [[:en:Kitano Tenmangū|Kitano Tenmangū]] to appease Michizane’s spirit.<ref>Karatanojin is now enshrined at the Hi no Miko Shrine within Kitano Tenmangū.</ref> Similarly, in Dazaifu, Michizane was worshipped at a mausoleum later developed into [[:en:Dazaifu Tenmangū|Dazaifu Tenmangū]] by order of Emperor Daigo and [[:en:Fujiwara_no_Nakahira|Fujiwara no Nakahira]]. Additionally, [[:en:Osaka Tenmangū|Osaka Tenmangū]] was built in 949 after seven pine trees mysteriously sprouted overnight at a site believed to be under Michizane’s protection.
During the late Heian and Kamakura periods, the belief in Michizane’s wrath waned. According to the ''Tenjin Engi'' (天神縁起), he became revered as a merciful deity of integrity, poetry, and the arts, granting longevity in this life and bliss in the next.<ref>As Michizane was identified with the Eleven-Faced Kannon Bosatsu.</ref> Over time, Tenjin also became a protector against maritime disasters and was worshiped by royalty, military leaders, and merchants as a deity for victory and national protection. In the Edo period, Michizane was popularly venerated as the god of learning due to his scholarly achievements.
== Origin == [[:en:Kitano Tenmangū|Kitano Tenmangū]] and [[:en:Dazaifu Tenmangū|Dazaifu Tenmangū]] were established independently, each claiming to be the birthplace of Tenjin Faith. Others, such as [[:en:Hōfu Tenmangū|Hōfu Tenmangū]] and {{ill|Yoki Tenman Shrine|ja|與喜天満神社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yoki Tenman Shrine|qq=}}, also assert being the oldest origin sites.
== Local Tenjin Faith == [[Image:MATSUYAMA_CASTLE(IYO),TENJIN_YAGURA.JPG|thumb|right|250px|The Tenjin Yagura at Matsuyama Castle, Iyo Province]] [[Image:Japanese crest Kaga Umebachi.svg|thumb|right|150px|The Kaga Umebachi crest of the Maeda clan]]
; Hokuriku In [[:en:Fukui Prefecture|Fukui Prefecture]] and [[:en:Toyama Prefecture|Toyama Prefecture]], Tenjin images are displayed in the alcove when a firstborn son is born. The tradition of offering flounder on January 25 is also observed. These practices were promoted by [[:en:Matsudaira Yoshinaga|Matsudaira Yoshinaga]] during the late Edo period.
== Tenjin and the Number 25 == Michizane’s birth and death anniversaries fall on the 25th of June and February, respectively, leading to monthly festivals on the 25th. This association persists even today with events like the {{ill|Hajime Tenjin|ja|始め天神|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hajime Tenjin|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} on January 25 and the {{ill|Shimai Tenjin|ja|終い天神|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shimai Tenjin|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} on December 25.
== Tenjin and Oxen == Numerous legends link Michizane with oxen, portraying them as his loyal protectors and companions. Due to these stories, oxen are considered messengers of the deity and are often depicted in Tenmangū shrines as reclining ox statues.

<!--Tenjin faith--><!--Tenjin faith--> [[Category:Faith]] [[Category:Tenjin (kami)]] [[Category:Goryō faith]] [[Category:Japanese folk religion]] [[Category:Shinbutsu shūgō]] <!--jawiki derived wikidata interwikis--> [[en:Tenjin cult]] [[ko:천신신앙]] {{wikidata link|Q66890725}}

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Tenjin cult]][[ja:天神信仰]][[ko:천신신앙]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q66890725]] Wikidata name: Tenjin faith [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:53, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Tenjin Faith''' (天神信仰) refers to the [[:en:Faith|faith]] in Tenjin, the [[:en:Raijin|Raijin]] (god of thunder) in [[:en:Japan|Japan]]. Specifically, it pertains to the [[:en:Shinto|Shinto]] belief system venerating {{ill|Sugawara no Michizane|1=Sugawara no Michizane|2=ja|3=菅原道真|ja|Sugawara no Michizane|1=Sugawara no Michizane|2=ja|3=菅原道真|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sugawara no Michizane|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q382005}} as "Tenjin-sama," revered as a deity for fear and prayer.
Originally, Tenjin referred to {{ill|Amatsukami|ja|天津神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/matsukami|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 天津神・国津神|comment=confirming this goes to Amatsukami|1=Amatsukami|2=zh|3=天神 (日本神話)|4=fr|5=Amatsukami|6=mni|7=ꯑꯃꯠꯁꯨꯀꯃꯤ|comment=confirming this goes to Amatsukami|1=Amatsukami|2=zh|3=天神 (日本神話)|4=fr|5=Amatsukami|6=mni|7=ꯑꯃꯠꯁꯨꯀꯃꯤ}} as opposed to {{ill|Kunitsukami|ja|国津神|1=Kunitsukami|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kunitsukami|13=User:Immanuelle/Kunitsukami|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 天津神・国津神|comment=confirming this goes to Kunitsukami|1=Kunitsukami|2=zh|3=國神|4=fr|5=Kunitsukami|6=mni|7=ꯀꯨꯅꯤꯠꯁꯨꯀꯃꯤ}}, and it was not the name of a specific deity. However, shortly after Michizane’s death, he was enshrined as the deity '''[[:en:Tenjin (kami)|Tenman Daijizai Tenjin]]''' (てんまんだいじざいてんじん). Following the {{ill|Seiryōden lightning strike incident|ja|清涼殿落雷事件|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Seiryōden lightning strike incident|qq=}}, Michizane’s vengeful spirit was associated with the local [[:en:Yakusanoikazuchi|Yakusanoikazuchi]], leading to his recognition as '''Karatentenjin''' (火雷天神). Later, Karatanojin was incorporated as a retinue spirit under titles like '''Nihon Daijō Itokuten''' (日本太政威徳天 / にほんだじょういとくてん) and even '''Jitsudō Gongen''' (実道権現)<ref>Cited in the Tenjinkyō (天神経), transmitted through terakoya (寺子屋).</ref>. Michizane’s spirit was further venerated in Buddhist texts such as ''Watokō Tenjin'' (渡唐天神), ''Myōhō Tenjinkyō'' (妙法天神経), and ''Tenjinkyō'' (天神経).<ref>Initially, Tenjin faith was closely tied to Buddhism, as monks from both the Tendai and Shingon schools were involved in its development. Temples and pagodas were often built on shrine grounds until the [[:en:Haibutsu kishaku|Haibutsu kishaku]] movement during the Meiji era.</ref> This veneration became widely known as Tenjin Faith.
[[Image:Gagyu(sumatsunasiki tenmangu).JPG|thumb|right|270px|The reclining ox at Suma Tsunashiki Tenmangu, Kobe City]]
== History == Sugawara no Michizane was demoted and exiled to [[:en:Dazaifu (government)|Dazaifu]] due to the conspiracy of [[:en:Fujiwara no Tokihira|Fujiwara no Tokihira]], dying there in despair.
After his death, Michizane’s vassal {{ill|Aji Sake Yasuyuki|ja|味酒安行|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Aji Sake Yasuyuki|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} enshrined him as the deity '''[[:en:Tenjin (kami)|Tenman Daijizai Tenjin]]'''.
Subsequently, an outbreak of plagues, droughts, and the deaths of [[:en:Emperor Daigo|Emperor Daigo]]'s princes caused fear of Michizane’s curse. The {{ill|Seiryōden lightning strike incident|ja|清涼殿落雷事件|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Seiryōden lightning strike incident|qq=}} further linked Michizane’s spirit with the thunder god. The court pardoned Michizane’s alleged crimes and posthumously elevated his Rank. In Kyoto’s {{ill|Kitano|ja|北野 (京都市)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kitano|1=Kitano|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11404253}}, Karatanojin, a local [[:en:Landlord deity|landlord deity]], was enshrined as part of [[:en:Kitano Tenmangū|Kitano Tenmangū]] to appease Michizane’s spirit.<ref>Karatanojin is now enshrined at the Hi no Miko Shrine within Kitano Tenmangū.</ref> Similarly, in Dazaifu, Michizane was worshipped at a mausoleum later developed into [[:en:Dazaifu Tenmangū|Dazaifu Tenmangū]] by order of Emperor Daigo and [[:en:Fujiwara_no_Nakahira|Fujiwara no Nakahira]]. Additionally, [[:en:Osaka Tenmangū|Osaka Tenmangū]] was built in 949 after seven pine trees mysteriously sprouted overnight at a site believed to be under Michizane’s protection.
During the late Heian and Kamakura periods, the belief in Michizane’s wrath waned. In the Edo period, Michizane was popularly venerated as the god of learning due to his scholarly achievements.
== Origin == [[:en:Kitano Tenmangū|Kitano Tenmangū]] and [[:en:Dazaifu Tenmangū|Dazaifu Tenmangū]] were established independently, each claiming to be the birthplace of Tenjin Faith. Others, such as [[:en:Hōfu Tenmangū|Hōfu Tenmangū]] and {{ill|Yoki Tenman Shrine|ja|與喜天満神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yoki Tenman Shrine|qq=}}, also assert being the oldest origin sites.
== Local Tenjin Faith == [[Image:MATSUYAMA_CASTLE(IYO),TENJIN_YAGURA.JPG|thumb|right|250px|The Tenjin Yagura at Matsuyama Castle, Iyo Province]] [[Image:Japanese crest Kaga Umebachi.svg|thumb|right|150px|The Kaga Umebachi crest of the Maeda clan]]
; Hokuriku In [[:en:Fukui Prefecture|Fukui Prefecture]] and [[:en:Toyama Prefecture|Toyama Prefecture]], Tenjin images are displayed in the alcove when a firstborn son is born. The tradition of offering flounder on January 25 is also observed. These practices were promoted by [[:en:Matsudaira Yoshinaga|Matsudaira Yoshinaga]] during the late Edo period.
== Tenjin and the Number 25 == Michizane’s birth and death anniversaries fall on the 25th of June and February, respectively, leading to monthly festivals on the 25th. This association persists even today with events like the {{ill|Hajime Tenjin|ja|始め天神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hajime Tenjin|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} on January 25 and the {{ill|Shimai Tenjin|ja|終い天神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shimai Tenjin|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} on December 25.
== Tenjin and Oxen == Numerous legends link Michizane with oxen, portraying them as his loyal protectors and companions. Due to these stories, oxen are considered messengers of the deity and are often depicted in Tenmangū shrines as reclining ox statues.

== Tenjin and Oxen == Numerous legends link Michizane with oxen, portraying them as his loyal protectors and companions. Due to these stories, oxen are considered messengers of the deity and are often depicted in Tenmangū shrines as reclining ox statues.
== References == {{Reflist|2}}

revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Tenjin cult]][[ja:天神信仰]][[ko:천신신앙]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q66890725]] Wikidata name: Tenjin faith [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:53, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Tenjin Faith''' (天神信仰) refers to the [[:en:Faith|faith]] in Tenjin, the [[:en:Raijin|Raijin]] (god of thunder) in [[:en:Japan|Japan]]. Specifically, it pertains to the [[:en:Shinto|Shinto]] belief system venerating {{ill|Sugawara no Michizane|1=Sugawara no Michizane|2=ja|3=菅原道真|ja|Sugawara no Michizane|1=Sugawara no Michizane|2=ja|3=菅原道真|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sugawara no Michizane|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q382005}} as "Tenjin-sama," revered as a deity for fear and prayer.
Originally, Tenjin referred to {{ill|Amatsukami|ja|天津神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/matsukami|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 天津神・国津神|comment=confirming this goes to Amatsukami|1=Amatsukami|2=zh|3=天神 (日本神話)|4=fr|5=Amatsukami|6=mni|7=ꯑꯃꯠꯁꯨꯀꯃꯤ|comment=confirming this goes to Amatsukami|1=Amatsukami|2=zh|3=天神 (日本神話)|4=fr|5=Amatsukami|6=mni|7=ꯑꯃꯠꯁꯨꯀꯃꯤ}} as opposed to {{ill|Kunitsukami|ja|国津神|1=Kunitsukami|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kunitsukami|13=User:Immanuelle/Kunitsukami|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 天津神・国津神|comment=confirming this goes to Kunitsukami|1=Kunitsukami|2=zh|3=國神|4=fr|5=Kunitsukami|6=mni|7=ꯀꯨꯅꯤꯠꯁꯨꯀꯃꯤ}}, and it was not the name of a specific deity. However, shortly after Michizane’s death, he was enshrined as the deity '''[[:en:Tenjin (kami)|Tenman Daijizai Tenjin]]''' (てんまんだいじざいてんじん). Following the {{ill|Seiryōden lightning strike incident|ja|清涼殿落雷事件|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Seiryōden lightning strike incident|qq=}}, Michizane’s vengeful spirit was associated with the local [[:en:Yakusanoikazuchi|Yakusanoikazuchi]], leading to his recognition as '''Karatentenjin''' (火雷天神). Later, Karatanojin was incorporated as a retinue spirit under titles like '''Nihon Daijō Itokuten''' (日本太政威徳天 / にほんだじょういとくてん) and even '''Jitsudō Gongen''' (実道権現)<ref>Cited in the Tenjinkyō (天神経), transmitted through terakoya (寺子屋).</ref>. Michizane’s spirit was further venerated in Buddhist texts such as ''Watokō Tenjin'' (渡唐天神), ''Myōhō Tenjinkyō'' (妙法天神経), and ''Tenjinkyō'' (天神経).<ref>Initially, Tenjin faith was closely tied to Buddhism, as monks from both the Tendai and Shingon schools were involved in its development. Temples and pagodas were often built on shrine grounds until the [[:en:Haibutsu kishaku|Haibutsu kishaku]] movement during the Meiji era.</ref> This veneration became widely known as Tenjin Faith.
[[Image:Gagyu(sumatsunasiki tenmangu).JPG|thumb|right|270px|The reclining ox at Suma Tsunashiki Tenmangu, Kobe City]]
== History == Sugawara no Michizane was demoted and exiled to [[:en:Dazaifu (government)|Dazaifu]] due to the conspiracy of [[:en:Fujiwara no Tokihira|Fujiwara no Tokihira]], dying there in despair.
After his death, Michizane’s vassal {{ill|Aji Sake Yasuyuki|ja|味酒安行|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Aji Sake Yasuyuki|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} enshrined him as the deity '''[[:en:Tenjin (kami)|Tenman Daijizai Tenjin]]'''.
Subsequently, an outbreak of plagues, droughts, and the deaths of [[:en:Emperor Daigo|Emperor Daigo]]'s princes caused fear of Michizane’s curse. The {{ill|Seiryōden lightning strike incident|ja|清涼殿落雷事件|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Seiryōden lightning strike incident|qq=}} further linked Michizane’s spirit with the thunder god. The court pardoned Michizane’s alleged crimes and posthumously elevated his Rank. In Kyoto’s {{ill|Kitano|ja|北野 (京都市)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kitano|1=Kitano|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11404253}}, Karatanojin, a local [[:en:Landlord deity|landlord deity]], was enshrined as part of [[:en:Kitano Tenmangū|Kitano Tenmangū]] to appease Michizane’s spirit.<ref>Karatanojin is now enshrined at the Hi no Miko Shrine within Kitano Tenmangū.</ref> Similarly, in Dazaifu, Michizane was worshipped at a mausoleum later developed into [[:en:Dazaifu Tenmangū|Dazaifu Tenmangū]] by order of Emperor Daigo and [[:en:Fujiwara_no_Nakahira|Fujiwara no Nakahira]]. Additionally, [[:en:Osaka Tenmangū|Osaka Tenmangū]] was built in 949 after seven pine trees mysteriously sprouted overnight at a site believed to be under Michizane’s protection.
During the late Heian and Kamakura periods, the belief in Michizane’s wrath waned. According to the ''Tenjin Engi'' (天神縁起), he became revered as a merciful deity of integrity, poetry, and the arts, granting longevity in this life and bliss in the next.<ref>As Michizane was identified with the Eleven-Faced Kannon Bosatsu.</ref> Over time, Tenjin also became a protector against maritime disasters and was worshiped by royalty, military leaders, and merchants as a deity for victory and national protection. In the Edo period, Michizane was popularly venerated as the god of learning due to his scholarly achievements.
== Origin == [[:en:Kitano Tenmangū|Kitano Tenmangū]] and [[:en:Dazaifu Tenmangū|Dazaifu Tenmangū]] were established independently, each claiming to be the birthplace of Tenjin Faith. Others, such as [[:en:Hōfu Tenmangū|Hōfu Tenmangū]] and {{ill|Yoki Tenman Shrine|ja|與喜天満神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yoki Tenman Shrine|qq=}}, also assert being the oldest origin sites.
== Local Tenjin Faith == [[Image:MATSUYAMA_CASTLE(IYO),TENJIN_YAGURA.JPG|thumb|right|250px|The Tenjin Yagura at Matsuyama Castle, Iyo Province]] [[Image:Japanese crest Kaga Umebachi.svg|thumb|right|150px|The Kaga Umebachi crest of the Maeda clan]]
; Hokuriku In [[:en:Fukui Prefecture|Fukui Prefecture]] and [[:en:Toyama Prefecture|Toyama Prefecture]], Tenjin images are displayed in the alcove when a firstborn son is born. The tradition of offering flounder on January 25 is also observed. These practices were promoted by [[:en:Matsudaira Yoshinaga|Matsudaira Yoshinaga]] during the late Edo period.
== Tenjin and the Number 25 == Michizane’s birth and death anniversaries fall on the 25th of June and February, respectively, leading to monthly festivals on the 25th. This association persists even today with events like the {{ill|Hajime Tenjin|ja|始め天神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hajime Tenjin|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} on January 25 and the {{ill|Shimai Tenjin|ja|終い天神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shimai Tenjin|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} on December 25.
== Tenjin and Oxen == Numerous legends link Michizane with oxen, portraying them as his loyal protectors and companions. Due to these stories, oxen are considered messengers of the deity and are often depicted in Tenmangū shrines as reclining ox statues.
== References == {{Reflist|2}}

== External Links == {{Shinto Portal|[[Image:Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|40px]]}} * [http://www.jinjahoncho.or.jp/2007/02/01%E3%80%80%E5%A4%A9%E7%A5%9E%E3%81%95%E3%81%BE/ Tenjin-sama - Jinja Honchō]{{Dead link|date=August 2024}} * [http://www.tokyo-jinjacho.or.jp/syoukai/21_itabashi/21021.html Kitano Shrine, Tokumaru, Itabashi Ward, Tokyo]

Specifically, it pertains to the [[:en:Shinto|Shinto]] belief system venerating {{ill|Sugawara no Michizane|1=Sugawara no Michizane|2=ja|3=菅原道真|ja|Sugawara no Michizane|1=Sugawara no Michizane|2=ja|3=菅原道真|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sugawara no Michizane|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q382005}} as "Tenjin-sama," revered as a deity for fear and prayer.
Originally, Tenjin referred to {{ill|Amatsukami|ja|天津神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/matsukami|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 天津神・国津神|comment=confirming this goes to Amatsukami|1=Amatsukami|2=zh|3=天神 (日本神話)|4=fr|5=Amatsukami|6=mni|7=ꯑꯃꯠꯁꯨꯀꯃꯤ|comment=confirming this goes to Amatsukami|1=Amatsukami|2=zh|3=天神 (日本神話)|4=fr|5=Amatsukami|6=mni|7=ꯑꯃꯠꯁꯨꯀꯃꯤ}} as opposed to {{ill|Kunitsukami|ja|国津神|1=Kunitsukami|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kunitsukami|13=User:Immanuelle/Kunitsukami|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 天津神・国津神|comment=confirming this goes to Kunitsukami|1=Kunitsukami|2=zh|3=國神|4=fr|5=Kunitsukami|6=mni|7=ꯀꯨꯅꯤꯠꯁꯨꯀꯃꯤ}}, and it was not the name of a specific deity. However, shortly after Michizane’s death, he was enshrined as the deity '''[[:en:Tenjin (kami)|Tenman Daijizai Tenjin]]''' (てんまんだいじざいてんじん). Following the {{ill|Seiryōden lightning strike incident|ja|清涼殿落雷事件|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Seiryōden lightning strike incident|qq=}}, Michizane’s vengeful spirit was associated with the local [[:en:Yakusanoikazuchi|Yakusanoikazuchi]], leading to his recognition as '''Karatentenjin''' (火雷天神). Later, Karatanojin was incorporated as a retinue spirit under titles like '''Nihon Daijō Itokuten''' (日本太政威徳天 / にほんだじょういとくてん) and even '''Jitsudō Gongen''' (実道権現)<ref>Cited in the Tenjinkyō (天神経), transmitted through terakoya (寺子屋).</ref>. Michizane’s spirit was further venerated in Buddhist texts such as ''Watokō Tenjin'' (渡唐天神), ''Myōhō Tenjinkyō'' (妙法天神経), and ''Tenjinkyō'' (天神経).<ref>Initially, Tenjin faith was closely tied to Buddhism, as monks from both the Tendai and Shingon schools were involved in its development. Temples and pagodas were often built on shrine grounds until the [[:en:Haibutsu kishaku|Haibutsu kishaku]] movement during the Meiji era.</ref> This veneration became widely known as Tenjin Faith.
[[Image:Gagyu(sumatsunasiki tenmangu).JPG|thumb|right|270px|The reclining ox at Suma Tsunashiki Tenmangu, Kobe City]]
== History == Sugawara no Michizane was demoted and exiled to [[:en:Dazaifu (government)|Dazaifu]] due to the conspiracy of [[:en:Fujiwara no Tokihira|Fujiwara no Tokihira]], dying there in despair.
After his death, Michizane’s vassal {{ill|Aji Sake Yasuyuki|ja|味酒安行|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Aji Sake Yasuyuki|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} enshrined him as the deity '''[[:en:Tenjin (kami)|Tenman Daijizai Tenjin]]'''.
Subsequently, an outbreak of plagues, droughts, and the deaths of [[:en:Emperor Daigo|Emperor Daigo]]'s princes caused fear of Michizane’s curse. The {{ill|Seiryōden lightning strike incident|ja|清涼殿落雷事件|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Seiryōden lightning strike incident|qq=}} further linked Michizane’s spirit with the thunder god. The court pardoned Michizane’s alleged crimes and posthumously elevated his Rank. In Kyoto’s {{ill|Kitano|ja|北野 (京都市)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kitano|1=Kitano|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11404253}}, Karatanojin, a local [[:en:Landlord deity|landlord deity]], was enshrined as part of [[:en:Kitano Tenmangū|Kitano Tenmangū]] to appease Michizane’s spirit.<ref>Karatanojin is now enshrined at the Hi no Miko Shrine within Kitano Tenmangū.</ref> Similarly, in Dazaifu, Michizane was worshipped at a mausoleum later developed into [[:en:Dazaifu Tenmangū|Dazaifu Tenmangū]] by order of Emperor Daigo and [[:en:Fujiwara_no_Nakahira|Fujiwara no Nakahira]]. Additionally, [[:en:Osaka Tenmangū|Osaka Tenmangū]] was built in 949 after seven pine trees mysteriously sprouted overnight at a site believed to be under Michizane’s protection.
During the late Heian and Kamakura periods, the belief in Michizane’s wrath waned. According to the ''Tenjin Engi'' (天神縁起), he became revered as a merciful deity of integrity, poetry, and the arts, granting longevity in this life and bliss in the next.<ref>As Michizane was identified with the Eleven-Faced Kannon Bosatsu.</ref> Over time, Tenjin also became a protector against maritime disasters and was worshiped by royalty, military leaders, and merchants as a deity for victory and national protection. In the Edo period, Michizane was popularly venerated as the god of learning due to his scholarly achievements.
== Origin == [[:en:Kitano Tenmangū|Kitano Tenmangū]] and [[:en:Dazaifu Tenmangū|Dazaifu Tenmangū]] were established independently, each claiming to be the birthplace of Tenjin Faith. Others, such as [[:en:Hōfu Tenmangū|Hōfu Tenmangū]] and {{ill|Yoki Tenman Shrine|ja|與喜天満神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yoki Tenman Shrine|qq=}}, also assert being the oldest origin sites.
== Local Tenjin Faith == [[Image:MATSUYAMA_CASTLE(IYO),TENJIN_YAGURA.JPG|thumb|right|250px|The Tenjin Yagura at Matsuyama Castle, Iyo Province]] [[Image:Japanese crest Kaga Umebachi.svg|thumb|right|150px|The Kaga Umebachi crest of the Maeda clan]]
; Hokuriku In [[:en:Fukui Prefecture|Fukui Prefecture]] and [[:en:Toyama Prefecture|Toyama Prefecture]], Tenjin images are displayed in the alcove when a firstborn son is born. The tradition of offering flounder on January 25 is also observed. These practices were promoted by [[:en:Matsudaira Yoshinaga|Matsudaira Yoshinaga]] during the late Edo period.
== Tenjin and the Number 25 == Michizane’s birth and death anniversaries fall on the 25th of June and February, respectively, leading to monthly festivals on the 25th. This association persists even today with events like the {{ill|Hajime Tenjin|ja|始め天神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hajime Tenjin|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} on January 25 and the {{ill|Shimai Tenjin|ja|終い天神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shimai Tenjin|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} on December 25.
== Tenjin and Oxen == Numerous legends link Michizane with oxen, portraying them as his loyal protectors and companions. Due to these stories, oxen are considered messengers of the deity and are often depicted in Tenmangū shrines as reclining ox statues.

The {{ill|Seiryōden lightning strike incident|ja|清涼殿落雷事件|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Seiryōden lightning strike incident|qq=}} further linked Michizane’s spirit with the thunder god. The court pardoned Michizane’s alleged crimes and posthumously elevated his Rank. In Kyoto’s {{ill|Kitano|ja|北野 (京都市)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kitano|1=Kitano|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11404253}}, Karatanojin, a local [[:en:Landlord deity|landlord deity]], was enshrined as part of [[:en:Kitano Tenmangū|Kitano Tenmangū]] to appease Michizane’s spirit.<ref>Karatanojin is now enshrined at the Hi no Miko Shrine within Kitano Tenmangū.</ref> Similarly, in Dazaifu, Michizane was worshipped at a mausoleum later developed into [[:en:Dazaifu Tenmangū|Dazaifu Tenmangū]] by order of Emperor Daigo and [[:en:Fujiwara_no_Nakahira|Fujiwara no Nakahira]]. Additionally, [[:en:Osaka Tenmangū|Osaka Tenmangū]] was built in 949 after seven pine trees mysteriously sprouted overnight at a site believed to be under Michizane’s protection.
During the late Heian and Kamakura periods, the belief in Michizane’s wrath waned. According to the ''Tenjin Engi'' (天神縁起), he became revered as a merciful deity of integrity, poetry, and the arts, granting longevity in this life and bliss in the next.<ref>As Michizane was identified with the Eleven-Faced Kannon Bosatsu.</ref> Over time, Tenjin also became a protector against maritime disasters and was worshiped by royalty, military leaders, and merchants as a deity for victory and national protection. In the Edo period, Michizane was popularly venerated as the god of learning due to his scholarly achievements.
== Origin == [[:en:Kitano Tenmangū|Kitano Tenmangū]] and [[:en:Dazaifu Tenmangū|Dazaifu Tenmangū]] were established independently, each claiming to be the birthplace of Tenjin Faith. Others, such as [[:en:Hōfu Tenmangū|Hōfu Tenmangū]] and {{ill|Yoki Tenman Shrine|ja|與喜天満神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yoki Tenman Shrine|qq=}}, also assert being the oldest origin sites.
== Local Tenjin Faith == [[Image:MATSUYAMA_CASTLE(IYO),TENJIN_YAGURA.JPG|thumb|right|250px|The Tenjin Yagura at Matsuyama Castle, Iyo Province]] [[Image:Japanese crest Kaga Umebachi.svg|thumb|right|150px|The Kaga Umebachi crest of the Maeda clan]]
; Hokuriku In [[:en:Fukui Prefecture|Fukui Prefecture]] and [[:en:Toyama Prefecture|Toyama Prefecture]], Tenjin images are displayed in the alcove when a firstborn son is born. The tradition of offering flounder on January 25 is also observed. These practices were promoted by [[:en:Matsudaira Yoshinaga|Matsudaira Yoshinaga]] during the late Edo period.
== Tenjin and the Number 25 == Michizane’s birth and death anniversaries fall on the 25th of June and February, respectively, leading to monthly festivals on the 25th. This association persists even today with events like the {{ill|Hajime Tenjin|ja|始め天神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hajime Tenjin|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} on January 25 and the {{ill|Shimai Tenjin|ja|終い天神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shimai Tenjin|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} on December 25.
== Tenjin and Oxen == Numerous legends link Michizane with oxen, portraying them as his loyal protectors and companions. Due to these stories, oxen are considered messengers of the deity and are often depicted in Tenmangū shrines as reclining ox statues.

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Tenjin cult]][[ja:天神信仰]][[ko:천신신앙]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q66890725]] Wikidata name: Tenjin faith [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:53, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Tenjin Faith''' (天神信仰) refers to the [[:en:Faith|faith]] in Tenjin, the [[:en:Raijin|Raijin]] (god of thunder) in [[:en:Japan|Japan]]. Specifically, it pertains to the [[:en:Shinto|Shinto]] belief system venerating {{ill|Sugawara no Michizane|1=Sugawara no Michizane|2=ja|3=菅原道真|ja|Sugawara no Michizane|1=Sugawara no Michizane|2=ja|3=菅原道真|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sugawara no Michizane|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q382005}} as "Tenjin-sama," revered as a deity for fear and prayer.
Originally, Tenjin referred to {{ill|Amatsukami|ja|天津神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/matsukami|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 天津神・国津神|comment=confirming this goes to Amatsukami|1=Amatsukami|2=zh|3=天神 (日本神話)|4=fr|5=Amatsukami|6=mni|7=ꯑꯃꯠꯁꯨꯀꯃꯤ|comment=confirming this goes to Amatsukami|1=Amatsukami|2=zh|3=天神 (日本神話)|4=fr|5=Amatsukami|6=mni|7=ꯑꯃꯠꯁꯨꯀꯃꯤ}} as opposed to {{ill|Kunitsukami|ja|国津神|1=Kunitsukami|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kunitsukami|13=User:Immanuelle/Kunitsukami|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 天津神・国津神|comment=confirming this goes to Kunitsukami|1=Kunitsukami|2=zh|3=國神|4=fr|5=Kunitsukami|6=mni|7=ꯀꯨꯅꯤꯠꯁꯨꯀꯃꯤ}}, and it was not the name of a specific deity. However, shortly after Michizane’s death, he was enshrined as the deity '''[[:en:Tenjin (kami)|Tenman Daijizai Tenjin]]''' (てんまんだいじざいてんじん). Following the {{ill|Seiryōden lightning strike incident|ja|清涼殿落雷事件|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Seiryōden lightning strike incident|qq=}}, Michizane’s vengeful spirit was associated with the local [[:en:Yakusanoikazuchi|Yakusanoikazuchi]], leading to his recognition as '''Karatentenjin''' (火雷天神). Later, Karatanojin was incorporated as a retinue spirit under titles like '''Nihon Daijō Itokuten''' (日本太政威徳天 / にほんだじょういとくてん) and even '''Jitsudō Gongen''' (実道権現)<ref>Cited in the Tenjinkyō (天神経), transmitted through terakoya (寺子屋).</ref>. Michizane’s spirit was further venerated in Buddhist texts such as ''Watokō Tenjin'' (渡唐天神), ''Myōhō Tenjinkyō'' (妙法天神経), and ''Tenjinkyō'' (天神経).<ref>Initially, Tenjin faith was closely tied to Buddhism, as monks from both the Tendai and Shingon schools were involved in its development. Temples and pagodas were often built on shrine grounds until the [[:en:Haibutsu kishaku|Haibutsu kishaku]] movement during the Meiji era.</ref> This veneration became widely known as Tenjin Faith.
[[Image:Gagyu(sumatsunasiki tenmangu).JPG|thumb|right|270px|The reclining ox at Suma Tsunashiki Tenmangu, Kobe City]]
== History == Sugawara no Michizane was demoted and exiled to [[:en:Dazaifu (government)|Dazaifu]] due to the conspiracy of [[:en:Fujiwara no Tokihira|Fujiwara no Tokihira]], dying there in despair.
After his death, Michizane’s vassal {{ill|Aji Sake Yasuyuki|ja|味酒安行|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Aji Sake Yasuyuki|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} enshrined him as the deity '''[[:en:Tenjin (kami)|Tenman Daijizai Tenjin]]'''.
Subsequently, an outbreak of plagues, droughts, and the deaths of [[:en:Emperor Daigo|Emperor Daigo]]'s princes caused fear of Michizane’s curse. The {{ill|Seiryōden lightning strike incident|ja|清涼殿落雷事件|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Seiryōden lightning strike incident|qq=}} further linked Michizane’s spirit with the thunder god. The court pardoned Michizane’s alleged crimes and posthumously elevated his Rank. In Kyoto’s {{ill|Kitano|ja|北野 (京都市)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kitano|1=Kitano|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11404253}}, Karatanojin, a local [[:en:Landlord deity|landlord deity]], was enshrined as part of [[:en:Kitano Tenmangū|Kitano Tenmangū]] to appease Michizane’s spirit.<ref>Karatanojin is now enshrined at the Hi no Miko Shrine within Kitano Tenmangū.</ref> Similarly, in Dazaifu, Michizane was worshipped at a mausoleum later developed into [[:en:Dazaifu Tenmangū|Dazaifu Tenmangū]] by order of Emperor Daigo and [[:en:Fujiwara_no_Nakahira|Fujiwara no Nakahira]]. Additionally, [[:en:Osaka Tenmangū|Osaka Tenmangū]] was built in 949 after seven pine trees mysteriously sprouted overnight at a site believed to be under Michizane’s protection.
During the late Heian and Kamakura periods, the belief in Michizane’s wrath waned. According to the ''Tenjin Engi'' (天神縁起), he became revered as a merciful deity of integrity, poetry, and the arts, granting longevity in this life and bliss in the next.<ref>As Michizane was identified with the Eleven-Faced Kannon Bosatsu.</ref> Over time, Tenjin also became a protector against maritime disasters and was worshiped by royalty, military leaders, and merchants as a deity for victory and national protection. In the Edo period, Michizane was popularly venerated as the god of learning due to his scholarly achievements.
== Origin == [[:en:Kitano Tenmangū|Kitano Tenmangū]] and [[:en:Dazaifu Tenmangū|Dazaifu Tenmangū]] were established independently, each claiming to be the birthplace of Tenjin Faith. Others, such as [[:en:Hōfu Tenmangū|Hōfu Tenmangū]] and {{ill|Yoki Tenman Shrine|ja|與喜天満神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yoki Tenman Shrine|qq=}}, also assert being the oldest origin sites.
== Local Tenjin Faith == [[Image:MATSUYAMA_CASTLE(IYO),TENJIN_YAGURA.JPG|thumb|right|250px|The Tenjin Yagura at Matsuyama Castle, Iyo Province]] [[Image:Japanese crest Kaga Umebachi.svg|thumb|right|150px|The Kaga Umebachi crest of the Maeda clan]]
; Hokuriku In [[:en:Fukui Prefecture|Fukui Prefecture]] and [[:en:Toyama Prefecture|Toyama Prefecture]], Tenjin images are displayed in the alcove when a firstborn son is born. The tradition of offering flounder on January 25 is also observed. These practices were promoted by [[:en:Matsudaira Yoshinaga|Matsudaira Yoshinaga]] during the late Edo period.
== Tenjin and the Number 25 == Michizane’s birth and death anniversaries fall on the 25th of June and February, respectively, leading to monthly festivals on the 25th. This association persists even today with events like the {{ill|Hajime Tenjin|ja|始め天神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hajime Tenjin|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} on January 25 and the {{ill|Shimai Tenjin|ja|終い天神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shimai Tenjin|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} on December 25.
== Tenjin and Oxen == Numerous legends link Michizane with oxen, portraying them as his loyal protectors and companions. Due to these stories, oxen are considered messengers of the deity and are often depicted in Tenmangū shrines as reclining ox statues.

== Tenjin and Oxen == Numerous legends link Michizane with oxen, portraying them as his loyal protectors and companions. Due to these stories, oxen are considered messengers of the deity and are often depicted in Tenmangū shrines as reclining ox statues.
== References == {{Reflist|2}}

revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Tenjin cult]][[ja:天神信仰]][[ko:천신신앙]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q66890725]] Wikidata name: Tenjin faith [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:53, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Tenjin Faith''' (天神信仰) refers to the [[:en:Faith|faith]] in Tenjin, the [[:en:Raijin|Raijin]] (god of thunder) in [[:en:Japan|Japan]]. Specifically, it pertains to the [[:en:Shinto|Shinto]] belief system venerating {{ill|Sugawara no Michizane|1=Sugawara no Michizane|2=ja|3=菅原道真|ja|Sugawara no Michizane|1=Sugawara no Michizane|2=ja|3=菅原道真|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sugawara no Michizane|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q382005}} as "Tenjin-sama," revered as a deity for fear and prayer.
Originally, Tenjin referred to {{ill|Amatsukami|ja|天津神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/matsukami|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 天津神・国津神|comment=confirming this goes to Amatsukami|1=Amatsukami|2=zh|3=天神 (日本神話)|4=fr|5=Amatsukami|6=mni|7=ꯑꯃꯠꯁꯨꯀꯃꯤ|comment=confirming this goes to Amatsukami|1=Amatsukami|2=zh|3=天神 (日本神話)|4=fr|5=Amatsukami|6=mni|7=ꯑꯃꯠꯁꯨꯀꯃꯤ}} as opposed to {{ill|Kunitsukami|ja|国津神|1=Kunitsukami|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kunitsukami|13=User:Immanuelle/Kunitsukami|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 天津神・国津神|comment=confirming this goes to Kunitsukami|1=Kunitsukami|2=zh|3=國神|4=fr|5=Kunitsukami|6=mni|7=ꯀꯨꯅꯤꯠꯁꯨꯀꯃꯤ}}, and it was not the name of a specific deity. However, shortly after Michizane’s death, he was enshrined as the deity '''[[:en:Tenjin (kami)|Tenman Daijizai Tenjin]]''' (てんまんだいじざいてんじん). Following the {{ill|Seiryōden lightning strike incident|ja|清涼殿落雷事件|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Seiryōden lightning strike incident|qq=}}, Michizane’s vengeful spirit was associated with the local [[:en:Yakusanoikazuchi|Yakusanoikazuchi]], leading to his recognition as '''Karatentenjin''' (火雷天神). Later, Karatanojin was incorporated as a retinue spirit under titles like '''Nihon Daijō Itokuten''' (日本太政威徳天 / にほんだじょういとくてん) and even '''Jitsudō Gongen''' (実道権現)<ref>Cited in the Tenjinkyō (天神経), transmitted through terakoya (寺子屋).</ref>. Michizane’s spirit was further venerated in Buddhist texts such as ''Watokō Tenjin'' (渡唐天神), ''Myōhō Tenjinkyō'' (妙法天神経), and ''Tenjinkyō'' (天神経).<ref>Initially, Tenjin faith was closely tied to Buddhism, as monks from both the Tendai and Shingon schools were involved in its development. Temples and pagodas were often built on shrine grounds until the [[:en:Haibutsu kishaku|Haibutsu kishaku]] movement during the Meiji era.</ref> This veneration became widely known as Tenjin Faith.
[[Image:Gagyu(sumatsunasiki tenmangu).JPG|thumb|right|270px|The reclining ox at Suma Tsunashiki Tenmangu, Kobe City]]
== History == Sugawara no Michizane was demoted and exiled to [[:en:Dazaifu (government)|Dazaifu]] due to the conspiracy of [[:en:Fujiwara no Tokihira|Fujiwara no Tokihira]], dying there in despair.
After his death, Michizane’s vassal {{ill|Aji Sake Yasuyuki|ja|味酒安行|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Aji Sake Yasuyuki|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} enshrined him as the deity '''[[:en:Tenjin (kami)|Tenman Daijizai Tenjin]]'''.
Subsequently, an outbreak of plagues, droughts, and the deaths of [[:en:Emperor Daigo|Emperor Daigo]]'s princes caused fear of Michizane’s curse. The {{ill|Seiryōden lightning strike incident|ja|清涼殿落雷事件|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Seiryōden lightning strike incident|qq=}} further linked Michizane’s spirit with the thunder god. The court pardoned Michizane’s alleged crimes and posthumously elevated his Rank. In Kyoto’s {{ill|Kitano|ja|北野 (京都市)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kitano|1=Kitano|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11404253}}, Karatanojin, a local [[:en:Landlord deity|landlord deity]], was enshrined as part of [[:en:Kitano Tenmangū|Kitano Tenmangū]] to appease Michizane’s spirit.<ref>Karatanojin is now enshrined at the Hi no Miko Shrine within Kitano Tenmangū.</ref> Similarly, in Dazaifu, Michizane was worshipped at a mausoleum later developed into [[:en:Dazaifu Tenmangū|Dazaifu Tenmangū]] by order of Emperor Daigo and [[:en:Fujiwara_no_Nakahira|Fujiwara no Nakahira]]. Additionally, [[:en:Osaka Tenmangū|Osaka Tenmangū]] was built in 949 after seven pine trees mysteriously sprouted overnight at a site believed to be under Michizane’s protection.
During the late Heian and Kamakura periods, the belief in Michizane’s wrath waned. According to the ''Tenjin Engi'' (天神縁起), he became revered as a merciful deity of integrity, poetry, and the arts, granting longevity in this life and bliss in the next.<ref>As Michizane was identified with the Eleven-Faced Kannon Bosatsu.</ref> Over time, Tenjin also became a protector against maritime disasters and was worshiped by royalty, military leaders, and merchants as a deity for victory and national protection. In the Edo period, Michizane was popularly venerated as the god of learning due to his scholarly achievements.
== Origin == [[:en:Kitano Tenmangū|Kitano Tenmangū]] and [[:en:Dazaifu Tenmangū|Dazaifu Tenmangū]] were established independently, each claiming to be the birthplace of Tenjin Faith. Others, such as [[:en:Hōfu Tenmangū|Hōfu Tenmangū]] and {{ill|Yoki Tenman Shrine|ja|與喜天満神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yoki Tenman Shrine|qq=}}, also assert being the oldest origin sites.
== Local Tenjin Faith == [[Image:MATSUYAMA_CASTLE(IYO),TENJIN_YAGURA.JPG|thumb|right|250px|The Tenjin Yagura at Matsuyama Castle, Iyo Province]] [[Image:Japanese crest Kaga Umebachi.svg|thumb|right|150px|The Kaga Umebachi crest of the Maeda clan]]
; Hokuriku In [[:en:Fukui Prefecture|Fukui Prefecture]] and [[:en:Toyama Prefecture|Toyama Prefecture]], Tenjin images are displayed in the alcove when a firstborn son is born. The tradition of offering flounder on January 25 is also observed. These practices were promoted by [[:en:Matsudaira Yoshinaga|Matsudaira Yoshinaga]] during the late Edo period.
== Tenjin and the Number 25 == Michizane’s birth and death anniversaries fall on the 25th of June and February, respectively, leading to monthly festivals on the 25th. This association persists even today with events like the {{ill|Hajime Tenjin|ja|始め天神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hajime Tenjin|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} on January 25 and the {{ill|Shimai Tenjin|ja|終い天神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shimai Tenjin|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} on December 25.
== Tenjin and Oxen == Numerous legends link Michizane with oxen, portraying them as his loyal protectors and companions. Due to these stories, oxen are considered messengers of the deity and are often depicted in Tenmangū shrines as reclining ox statues.
== References == {{Reflist|2}}

== External Links == {{Shinto Portal|[[Image:Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|40px]]}} * [http://www.jinjahoncho.or.jp/2007/02/01%E3%80%80%E5%A4%A9%E7%A5%9E%E3%81%95%E3%81%BE/ Tenjin-sama - Jinja Honchō]{{Dead link|date=August 2024}} * [http://www.tokyo-jinjacho.or.jp/syoukai/21_itabashi/21021.html Kitano Shrine, Tokumaru, Itabashi Ward, Tokyo]

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Tenjin cult]][[ja:天神信仰]][[ko:천신신앙]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Tenjin cult]][[ja:天神信仰]][[ko:천신신앙]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Tenjin cult]][[ja:天神信仰]][[ko:천신신앙]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q66890725]] Wikidata name: Tenjin faith [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:53, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Tenjin Faith''' (天神信仰) refers to the [[:en:Faith|faith]] in Tenjin, the [[:en:Raijin|Raijin]] (god of thunder) in [[:en:Japan|Japan]]. Specifically, it pertains to the [[:en:Shinto|Shinto]] belief system venerating {{ill|Sugawara no Michizane|1=Sugawara no Michizane|2=ja|3=菅原道真|ja|Sugawara no Michizane|1=Sugawara no Michizane|2=ja|3=菅原道真|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sugawara no Michizane|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q382005}} as "Tenjin-sama," revered as a deity for fear and prayer.
Originally, Tenjin referred to {{ill|Amatsukami|ja|天津神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/matsukami|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 天津神・国津神|comment=confirming this goes to Amatsukami|1=Amatsukami|2=zh|3=天神 (日本神話)|4=fr|5=Amatsukami|6=mni|7=ꯑꯃꯠꯁꯨꯀꯃꯤ|comment=confirming this goes to Amatsukami|1=Amatsukami|2=zh|3=天神 (日本神話)|4=fr|5=Amatsukami|6=mni|7=ꯑꯃꯠꯁꯨꯀꯃꯤ}} as opposed to {{ill|Kunitsukami|ja|国津神|1=Kunitsukami|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kunitsukami|13=User:Immanuelle/Kunitsukami|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 天津神・国津神|comment=confirming this goes to Kunitsukami|1=Kunitsukami|2=zh|3=國神|4=fr|5=Kunitsukami|6=mni|7=ꯀꯨꯅꯤꯠꯁꯨꯀꯃꯤ}}, and it was not the name of a specific deity. However, shortly after Michizane’s death, he was enshrined as the deity '''[[:en:Tenjin (kami)|Tenman Daijizai Tenjin]]''' (てんまんだいじざいてんじん). Following the {{ill|Seiryōden lightning strike incident|ja|清涼殿落雷事件|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Seiryōden lightning strike incident|qq=}}, Michizane’s vengeful spirit was associated with the local [[:en:Yakusanoikazuchi|Yakusanoikazuchi]], leading to his recognition as '''Karatentenjin''' (火雷天神). Later, Karatanojin was incorporated as a retinue spirit under titles like '''Nihon Daijō Itokuten''' (日本太政威徳天 / にほんだじょういとくてん) and even '''Jitsudō Gongen''' (実道権現)<ref>Cited in the Tenjinkyō (天神経), transmitted through terakoya (寺子屋).</ref>. Michizane’s spirit was further venerated in Buddhist texts such as ''Watokō Tenjin'' (渡唐天神), ''Myōhō Tenjinkyō'' (妙法天神経), and ''Tenjinkyō'' (天神経).<ref>Initially, Tenjin faith was closely tied to Buddhism, as monks from both the Tendai and Shingon schools were involved in its development. Temples and pagodas were often built on shrine grounds until the [[:en:Haibutsu kishaku|Haibutsu kishaku]] movement during the Meiji era.</ref> This veneration became widely known as Tenjin Faith.
[[Image:Gagyu(sumatsunasiki tenmangu).JPG|thumb|right|270px|The reclining ox at Suma Tsunashiki Tenmangu, Kobe City]]
== History == Sugawara no Michizane was demoted and exiled to [[:en:Dazaifu (government)|Dazaifu]] due to the conspiracy of [[:en:Fujiwara no Tokihira|Fujiwara no Tokihira]], dying there in despair.
After his death, Michizane’s vassal {{ill|Aji Sake Yasuyuki|ja|味酒安行|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Aji Sake Yasuyuki|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} enshrined him as the deity '''[[:en:Tenjin (kami)|Tenman Daijizai Tenjin]]'''.
Subsequently, an outbreak of plagues, droughts, and the deaths of [[:en:Emperor Daigo|Emperor Daigo]]'s princes caused fear of Michizane’s curse. The {{ill|Seiryōden lightning strike incident|ja|清涼殿落雷事件|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Seiryōden lightning strike incident|qq=}} further linked Michizane’s spirit with the thunder god. The court pardoned Michizane’s alleged crimes and posthumously elevated his Rank. In Kyoto’s {{ill|Kitano|ja|北野 (京都市)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kitano|1=Kitano|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11404253}}, Karatanojin, a local [[:en:Landlord deity|landlord deity]], was enshrined as part of [[:en:Kitano Tenmangū|Kitano Tenmangū]] to appease Michizane’s spirit.<ref>Karatanojin is now enshrined at the Hi no Miko Shrine within Kitano Tenmangū.</ref> Similarly, in Dazaifu, Michizane was worshipped at a mausoleum later developed into [[:en:Dazaifu Tenmangū|Dazaifu Tenmangū]] by order of Emperor Daigo and [[:en:Fujiwara_no_Nakahira|Fujiwara no Nakahira]]. Additionally, [[:en:Osaka Tenmangū|Osaka Tenmangū]] was built in 949 after seven pine trees mysteriously sprouted overnight at a site believed to be under Michizane’s protection.
During the late Heian and Kamakura periods, the belief in Michizane’s wrath waned. According to the ''Tenjin Engi'' (天神縁起), he became revered as a merciful deity of integrity, poetry, and the arts, granting longevity in this life and bliss in the next.<ref>As Michizane was identified with the Eleven-Faced Kannon Bosatsu.</ref> Over time, Tenjin also became a protector against maritime disasters and was worshiped by royalty, military leaders, and merchants as a deity for victory and national protection. In the Edo period, Michizane was popularly venerated as the god of learning due to his scholarly achievements.
== Origin == [[:en:Kitano Tenmangū|Kitano Tenmangū]] and [[:en:Dazaifu Tenmangū|Dazaifu Tenmangū]] were established independently, each claiming to be the birthplace of Tenjin Faith. Others, such as [[:en:Hōfu Tenmangū|Hōfu Tenmangū]] and {{ill|Yoki Tenman Shrine|ja|與喜天満神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yoki Tenman Shrine|qq=}}, also assert being the oldest origin sites.
== Local Tenjin Faith == [[Image:MATSUYAMA_CASTLE(IYO),TENJIN_YAGURA.JPG|thumb|right|250px|The Tenjin Yagura at Matsuyama Castle, Iyo Province]] [[Image:Japanese crest Kaga Umebachi.svg|thumb|right|150px|The Kaga Umebachi crest of the Maeda clan]]
; Hokuriku In [[:en:Fukui Prefecture|Fukui Prefecture]] and [[:en:Toyama Prefecture|Toyama Prefecture]], Tenjin images are displayed in the alcove when a firstborn son is born. The tradition of offering flounder on January 25 is also observed. These practices were promoted by [[:en:Matsudaira Yoshinaga|Matsudaira Yoshinaga]] during the late Edo period.
== Tenjin and the Number 25 == Michizane’s birth and death anniversaries fall on the 25th of June and February, respectively, leading to monthly festivals on the 25th. This association persists even today with events like the {{ill|Hajime Tenjin|ja|始め天神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hajime Tenjin|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} on January 25 and the {{ill|Shimai Tenjin|ja|終い天神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shimai Tenjin|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} on December 25.
== Tenjin and Oxen == Numerous legends link Michizane with oxen, portraying them as his loyal protectors and companions. Due to these stories, oxen are considered messengers of the deity and are often depicted in Tenmangū shrines as reclining ox statues.
== References == {{Reflist|2}}

== External Links == {{Shinto Portal|[[Image:Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|40px]]}} * [http://www.jinjahoncho.or.jp/2007/02/01%E3%80%80%E5%A4%A9%E7%A5%9E%E3%81%95%E3%81%BE/ Tenjin-sama - Jinja Honchō]{{Dead link|date=August 2024}} * [http://www.tokyo-jinjacho.or.jp/syoukai/21_itabashi/21021.html Kitano Shrine, Tokumaru, Itabashi Ward, Tokyo]

; Hokuriku In [[:en:Fukui Prefecture|Fukui Prefecture]] and [[:en:Toyama Prefecture|Toyama Prefecture]], Tenjin images are displayed in the alcove when a firstborn son is born. [[Image:Gagyu(sumatsunasiki tenmangu).JPG|thumb|right|270px|The reclining ox at Suma Tsunashiki Tenmangu, Kobe City]]
== History == Sugawara no Michizane was demoted and exiled to [[:en:Dazaifu (government)|Dazaifu]] due to the conspiracy of [[:en:Fujiwara no Tokihira|Fujiwara no Tokihira]], dying there in despair.
After his death, Michizane’s vassal {{ill|Aji Sake Yasuyuki|ja|味酒安行|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Aji Sake Yasuyuki|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} enshrined him as the deity '''[[:en:Tenjin (kami)|Tenman Daijizai Tenjin]]'''.
Subsequently, an outbreak of plagues, droughts, and the deaths of [[:en:Emperor Daigo|Emperor Daigo]]'s princes caused fear of Michizane’s curse. The {{ill|Seiryōden lightning strike incident|ja|清涼殿落雷事件|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Seiryōden lightning strike incident|qq=}} further linked Michizane’s spirit with the thunder god. The court pardoned Michizane’s alleged crimes and posthumously elevated his Rank. In Kyoto’s {{ill|Kitano|ja|北野 (京都市)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kitano|1=Kitano|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11404253}}, Karatanojin, a local [[:en:Landlord deity|landlord deity]], was enshrined as part of [[:en:Kitano Tenmangū|Kitano Tenmangū]] to appease Michizane’s spirit.<ref>Karatanojin is now enshrined at the Hi no Miko Shrine within Kitano Tenmangū.</ref> Similarly, in Dazaifu, Michizane was worshipped at a mausoleum later developed into [[:en:Dazaifu Tenmangū|Dazaifu Tenmangū]] by order of Emperor Daigo and [[:en:Fujiwara_no_Nakahira|Fujiwara no Nakahira]]. Additionally, [[:en:Osaka Tenmangū|Osaka Tenmangū]] was built in 949 after seven pine trees mysteriously sprouted overnight at a site believed to be under Michizane’s protection.
During the late Heian and Kamakura periods, the belief in Michizane’s wrath waned. According to the ''Tenjin Engi'' (天神縁起), he became revered as a merciful deity of integrity, poetry, and the arts, granting longevity in this life and bliss in the next.<ref>As Michizane was identified with the Eleven-Faced Kannon Bosatsu.</ref> Over time, Tenjin also became a protector against maritime disasters and was worshiped by royalty, military leaders, and merchants as a deity for victory and national protection. In the Edo period, Michizane was popularly venerated as the god of learning due to his scholarly achievements.
== Origin == [[:en:Kitano Tenmangū|Kitano Tenmangū]] and [[:en:Dazaifu Tenmangū|Dazaifu Tenmangū]] were established independently, each claiming to be the birthplace of Tenjin Faith. Others, such as [[:en:Hōfu Tenmangū|Hōfu Tenmangū]] and {{ill|Yoki Tenman Shrine|ja|與喜天満神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yoki Tenman Shrine|qq=}}, also assert being the oldest origin sites.
== Local Tenjin Faith == [[Image:MATSUYAMA_CASTLE(IYO),TENJIN_YAGURA.JPG|thumb|right|250px|The Tenjin Yagura at Matsuyama Castle, Iyo Province]] [[Image:Japanese crest Kaga Umebachi.svg|thumb|right|150px|The Kaga Umebachi crest of the Maeda clan]]
; Hokuriku In [[:en:Fukui Prefecture|Fukui Prefecture]] and [[:en:Toyama Prefecture|Toyama Prefecture]], Tenjin images are displayed in the alcove when a firstborn son is born. The tradition of offering flounder on January 25 is also observed. These practices were promoted by [[:en:Matsudaira Yoshinaga|Matsudaira Yoshinaga]] during the late Edo period.
== Tenjin and the Number 25 == Michizane’s birth and death anniversaries fall on the 25th of June and February, respectively, leading to monthly festivals on the 25th. This association persists even today with events like the {{ill|Hajime Tenjin|ja|始め天神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hajime Tenjin|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} on January 25 and the {{ill|Shimai Tenjin|ja|終い天神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shimai Tenjin|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} on December 25.
== Tenjin and Oxen == Numerous legends link Michizane with oxen, portraying them as his loyal protectors and companions. Due to these stories, oxen are considered messengers of the deity and are often depicted in Tenmangū shrines as reclining ox statues.

{{wikidata link|Q66890725}} [[Category:Completion tier 1]] [[Category:Faith]] [[Category:Goryō faith]] [[Category:Japanese folk religion]] [[Category:pages with conflicting qids]] [[Category:Shinbutsu shūgō]] [[Category:Shinto]] [[Category:Sugawara no Michizane]] [[Category:Tenjin (kami)]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]] [[Category:Wikidata no short description]]

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Tenjin cult]][[ja:天神信仰]][[ko:천신신앙]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Tenjin cult]][[ja:天神信仰]][[ko:천신신앙]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Tenjin cult]][[ja:天神信仰]][[ko:천신신앙]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q66890725]] Wikidata name: Tenjin faith [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:53, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Tenjin Faith''' (天神信仰) refers to the [[:en:Faith|faith]] in Tenjin, the [[:en:Raijin|Raijin]] (god of thunder) in [[:en:Japan|Japan]]. Specifically, it pertains to the [[:en:Shinto|Shinto]] belief system venerating {{ill|Sugawara no Michizane|1=Sugawara no Michizane|2=ja|3=菅原道真|ja|Sugawara no Michizane|1=Sugawara no Michizane|2=ja|3=菅原道真|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sugawara no Michizane|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q382005}} as "Tenjin-sama," revered as a deity for fear and prayer.
Originally, Tenjin referred to {{ill|Amatsukami|ja|天津神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/matsukami|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 天津神・国津神|comment=confirming this goes to Amatsukami|1=Amatsukami|2=zh|3=天神 (日本神話)|4=fr|5=Amatsukami|6=mni|7=ꯑꯃꯠꯁꯨꯀꯃꯤ|comment=confirming this goes to Amatsukami|1=Amatsukami|2=zh|3=天神 (日本神話)|4=fr|5=Amatsukami|6=mni|7=ꯑꯃꯠꯁꯨꯀꯃꯤ}} as opposed to {{ill|Kunitsukami|ja|国津神|1=Kunitsukami|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kunitsukami|13=User:Immanuelle/Kunitsukami|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 天津神・国津神|comment=confirming this goes to Kunitsukami|1=Kunitsukami|2=zh|3=國神|4=fr|5=Kunitsukami|6=mni|7=ꯀꯨꯅꯤꯠꯁꯨꯀꯃꯤ}}, and it was not the name of a specific deity. However, shortly after Michizane’s death, he was enshrined as the deity '''[[:en:Tenjin (kami)|Tenman Daijizai Tenjin]]''' (てんまんだいじざいてんじん). Following the {{ill|Seiryōden lightning strike incident|ja|清涼殿落雷事件|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Seiryōden lightning strike incident|qq=}}, Michizane’s vengeful spirit was associated with the local [[:en:Yakusanoikazuchi|Yakusanoikazuchi]], leading to his recognition as '''Karatentenjin''' (火雷天神). Later, Karatanojin was incorporated as a retinue spirit under titles like '''Nihon Daijō Itokuten''' (日本太政威徳天 / にほんだじょういとくてん) and even '''Jitsudō Gongen''' (実道権現)<ref>Cited in the Tenjinkyō (天神経), transmitted through terakoya (寺子屋).</ref>. Michizane’s spirit was further venerated in Buddhist texts such as ''Watokō Tenjin'' (渡唐天神), ''Myōhō Tenjinkyō'' (妙法天神経), and ''Tenjinkyō'' (天神経).<ref>Initially, Tenjin faith was closely tied to Buddhism, as monks from both the Tendai and Shingon schools were involved in its development. Temples and pagodas were often built on shrine grounds until the [[:en:Haibutsu kishaku|Haibutsu kishaku]] movement during the Meiji era.</ref> This veneration became widely known as Tenjin Faith.
[[Image:Gagyu(sumatsunasiki tenmangu).JPG|thumb|right|270px|The reclining ox at Suma Tsunashiki Tenmangu, Kobe City]]

revision.comment: Moving from [[Category:Pages with conflicting qids]] to [[Category:Qq]] using [[c:Help:Cat-a-lot|Cat-a-lot]]
revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Tenjin cult]][[ja:天神信仰]][[ko:천신신앙]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Tenjin cult]][[ja:天神信仰]][[ko:천신신앙]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Tenjin cult]][[ja:天神信仰]][[ko:천신신앙]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q66890725]] Wikidata name: Tenjin faith [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:53, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Tenjin Faith''' (天神信仰) refers to the [[:en:Faith|faith]] in Tenjin, the [[:en:Raijin|Raijin]] (god of thunder) in [[:en:Japan|Japan]]. Specifically, it pertains to the [[:en:Shinto|Shinto]] belief system venerating {{ill|Sugawara no Michizane|1=Sugawara no Michizane|2=ja|3=菅原道真|ja|Sugawara no Michizane|1=Sugawara no Michizane|2=ja|3=菅原道真|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sugawara no Michizane|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q382005}} as "Tenjin-sama," revered as a deity for fear and prayer.
Originally, Tenjin referred to {{ill|Amatsukami|ja|天津神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/matsukami|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 天津神・国津神|comment=confirming this goes to Amatsukami|1=Amatsukami|2=zh|3=天神 (日本神話)|4=fr|5=Amatsukami|6=mni|7=ꯑꯃꯠꯁꯨꯀꯃꯤ|comment=confirming this goes to Amatsukami|1=Amatsukami|2=zh|3=天神 (日本神話)|4=fr|5=Amatsukami|6=mni|7=ꯑꯃꯠꯁꯨꯀꯃꯤ}} as opposed to {{ill|Kunitsukami|ja|国津神|1=Kunitsukami|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kunitsukami|13=User:Immanuelle/Kunitsukami|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 天津神・国津神|comment=confirming this goes to Kunitsukami|1=Kunitsukami|2=zh|3=國神|4=fr|5=Kunitsukami|6=mni|7=ꯀꯨꯅꯤꯠꯁꯨꯀꯃꯤ}}, and it was not the name of a specific deity. However, shortly after Michizane’s death, he was enshrined as the deity '''[[:en:Tenjin (kami)|Tenman Daijizai Tenjin]]''' (てんまんだいじざいてんじん). Following the {{ill|Seiryōden lightning strike incident|ja|清涼殿落雷事件|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Seiryōden lightning strike incident|qq=}}, Michizane’s vengeful spirit was associated with the local [[:en:Yakusanoikazuchi|Yakusanoikazuchi]], leading to his recognition as '''Karatentenjin''' (火雷天神). Later, Karatanojin was incorporated as a retinue spirit under titles like '''Nihon Daijō Itokuten''' (日本太政威徳天 / にほんだじょういとくてん) and even '''Jitsudō Gongen''' (実道権現)<ref>Cited in the Tenjinkyō (天神経), transmitted through terakoya (寺子屋).</ref>. Michizane’s spirit was further venerated in Buddhist texts such as ''Watokō Tenjin'' (渡唐天神), ''Myōhō Tenjinkyō'' (妙法天神経), and ''Tenjinkyō'' (天神経).<ref>Initially, Tenjin faith was closely tied to Buddhism, as monks from both the Tendai and Shingon schools were involved in its development. Temples and pagodas were often built on shrine grounds until the [[:en:Haibutsu kishaku|Haibutsu kishaku]] movement during the Meiji era.</ref> This veneration became widely known as Tenjin Faith.
[[Image:Gagyu(sumatsunasiki tenmangu).JPG|thumb|right|270px|The reclining ox at Suma Tsunashiki Tenmangu, Kobe City]]
== History == Sugawara no Michizane was demoted and exiled to [[:en:Dazaifu (government)|Dazaifu]] due to the conspiracy of [[:en:Fujiwara no Tokihira|Fujiwara no Tokihira]], dying there in despair.
After his death, Michizane’s vassal {{ill|Aji Sake Yasuyuki|ja|味酒安行|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Aji Sake Yasuyuki|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} enshrined him as the deity '''[[:en:Tenjin (kami)|Tenman Daijizai Tenjin]]'''.
Subsequently, an outbreak of plagues, droughts, and the deaths of [[:en:Emperor Daigo|Emperor Daigo]]'s princes caused fear of Michizane’s curse. The {{ill|Seiryōden lightning strike incident|ja|清涼殿落雷事件|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Seiryōden lightning strike incident|qq=}} further linked Michizane’s spirit with the thunder god. The court pardoned Michizane’s alleged crimes and posthumously elevated his Rank. In Kyoto’s {{ill|Kitano|ja|北野 (京都市)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kitano|1=Kitano|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11404253}}, Karatanojin, a local [[:en:Landlord deity|landlord deity]], was enshrined as part of [[:en:Kitano Tenmangū|Kitano Tenmangū]] to appease Michizane’s spirit.<ref>Karatanojin is now enshrined at the Hi no Miko Shrine within Kitano Tenmangū.</ref> Similarly, in Dazaifu, Michizane was worshipped at a mausoleum later developed into [[:en:Dazaifu Tenmangū|Dazaifu Tenmangū]] by order of Emperor Daigo and [[:en:Fujiwara_no_Nakahira|Fujiwara no Nakahira]]. Additionally, [[:en:Osaka Tenmangū|Osaka Tenmangū]] was built in 949 after seven pine trees mysteriously sprouted overnight at a site believed to be under Michizane’s protection.
During the late Heian and Kamakura periods, the belief in Michizane’s wrath waned. According to the ''Tenjin Engi'' (天神縁起), he became revered as a merciful deity of integrity, poetry, and the arts, granting longevity in this life and bliss in the next.<ref>As Michizane was identified with the Eleven-Faced Kannon Bosatsu.</ref> Over time, Tenjin also became a protector against maritime disasters and was worshiped by royalty, military leaders, and merchants as a deity for victory and national protection. In the Edo period, Michizane was popularly venerated as the god of learning due to his scholarly achievements.
== Origin == [[:en:Kitano Tenmangū|Kitano Tenmangū]] and [[:en:Dazaifu Tenmangū|Dazaifu Tenmangū]] were established independently, each claiming to be the birthplace of Tenjin Faith. Others, such as [[:en:Hōfu Tenmangū|Hōfu Tenmangū]] and {{ill|Yoki Tenman Shrine|ja|與喜天満神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yoki Tenman Shrine|qq=}}, also assert being the oldest origin sites.
== Local Tenjin Faith == [[Image:MATSUYAMA_CASTLE(IYO),TENJIN_YAGURA.JPG|thumb|right|250px|The Tenjin Yagura at Matsuyama Castle, Iyo Province]] [[Image:Japanese crest Kaga Umebachi.svg|thumb|right|150px|The Kaga Umebachi crest of the Maeda clan]]
; Hokuriku In [[:en:Fukui Prefecture|Fukui Prefecture]] and [[:en:Toyama Prefecture|Toyama Prefecture]], Tenjin images are displayed in the alcove when a firstborn son is born. The tradition of offering flounder on January 25 is also observed. These practices were promoted by [[:en:Matsudaira Yoshinaga|Matsudaira Yoshinaga]] during the late Edo period.
== Tenjin and the Number 25 == Michizane’s birth and death anniversaries fall on the 25th of June and February, respectively, leading to monthly festivals on the 25th. This association persists even today with events like the {{ill|Hajime Tenjin|ja|始め天神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hajime Tenjin|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} on January 25 and the {{ill|Shimai Tenjin|ja|終い天神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shimai Tenjin|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} on December 25.
== Tenjin and Oxen == Numerous legends link Michizane with oxen, portraying them as his loyal protectors and companions. Due to these stories, oxen are considered messengers of the deity and are often depicted in Tenmangū shrines as reclining ox statues.

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Tenjin cult]][[ja:天神信仰]][[ko:천신신앙]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Tenjin cult]][[ja:天神信仰]][[ko:천신신앙]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Tenjin cult]][[ja:天神信仰]][[ko:천신신앙]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q66890725]] Wikidata name: Tenjin faith [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:53, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Tenjin Faith''' (天神信仰) refers to the {{ill|Faith|en|Faith|WD=Q5410500|lt=faith}} in Tenjin, the {{ill|Raijin|en|Raijin|WD=Q12808082|lt=Raijin}} (god of thunder) in {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan}}. Specifically, it pertains to the {{ill|Shinto|en|Shinto|WD=Q812767|lt=Shinto}} belief system venerating {{ill|Sugawara no Michizane|1=Sugawara no Michizane|2=ja|3=菅原道真|ja|Sugawara no Michizane|1=Sugawara no Michizane|2=ja|3=菅原道真|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sugawara no Michizane|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q382005}} as "Tenjin-sama," revered as a deity for fear and prayer.
Originally, Tenjin referred to {{ill|Amatsukami|ja|天津神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/matsukami|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 天津神・国津神|comment=confirming this goes to Amatsukami|1=Amatsukami|2=zh|3=天神 (日本神話)|4=fr|5=Amatsukami|6=mni|7=ꯑꯃꯠꯁꯨꯀꯃꯤ|comment=confirming this goes to Amatsukami|1=Amatsukami|2=zh|3=天神 (日本神話)|4=fr|5=Amatsukami|6=mni|7=ꯑꯃꯠꯁꯨꯀꯃꯤ}} as opposed to {{ill|Kunitsukami|ja|国津神|1=Kunitsukami|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kunitsukami|13=User:Immanuelle/Kunitsukami|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 天津神・国津神|comment=confirming this goes to Kunitsukami|1=Kunitsukami|2=zh|3=國神|4=fr|5=Kunitsukami|6=mni|7=ꯀꯨꯅꯤꯠꯁꯨꯀꯃꯤ}}, and it was not the name of a specific deity. However, shortly after Michizane’s death, he was enshrined as the deity '''{{ill|Tenjin (kami)|en|Tenjin (kami)|WD=Q1753428|lt=Tenman Daijizai Tenjin}}''' (てんまんだいじざいてんじん). Following the {{ill|Seiryōden lightning strike incident|ja|清涼殿落雷事件|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Seiryōden lightning strike incident|qq=}}, Michizane’s vengeful spirit was associated with the local {{ill|Yakusanoikazuchi|en|Yakusanoikazuchi|WD=Q10892534|lt=Yakusanoikazuchi}}, leading to his recognition as '''Karatentenjin''' (火雷天神). Later, Karatanojin was incorporated as a retinue spirit under titles like '''Nihon Daijō Itokuten''' (日本太政威徳天 / にほんだじょういとくてん) and even '''Jitsudō Gongen''' (実道権現)<ref>Cited in the Tenjinkyō (天神経), transmitted through terakoya (寺子屋).</ref>. Michizane’s spirit was further venerated in Buddhist texts such as ''Watokō Tenjin'' (渡唐天神), ''Myōhō Tenjinkyō'' (妙法天神経), and ''Tenjinkyō'' (天神経).<ref>Initially, Tenjin faith was closely tied to Buddhism, as monks from both the Tendai and Shingon schools were involved in its development. Temples and pagodas were often built on shrine grounds until the {{ill|Haibutsu kishaku|en|Haibutsu kishaku|WD=Q702298|lt=Haibutsu kishaku}} movement during the Meiji era.</ref> This veneration became widely known as Tenjin Faith.
[[Image:Gagyu(sumatsunasiki tenmangu).JPG|thumb|right|270px|The reclining ox at Suma Tsunashiki Tenmangu, Kobe City]]
== History == Sugawara no Michizane was demoted and exiled to {{ill|Dazaifu (government)|en|Dazaifu (government)|WD=Q144845|lt=Dazaifu}} due to the conspiracy of {{ill|Fujiwara no Tokihira|en|Fujiwara no Tokihira|WD=Q2994735|lt=Fujiwara no Tokihira}}, dying there in despair.
After his death, Michizane’s vassal {{ill|Aji Sake Yasuyuki|ja|味酒安行|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Aji Sake Yasuyuki|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} enshrined him as the deity '''{{ill|Tenjin (kami)|en|Tenjin (kami)|WD=Q1753428|lt=Tenman Daijizai Tenjin}}'''.
Subsequently, an outbreak of plagues, droughts, and the deaths of {{ill|Emperor Daigo|en|Emperor Daigo|WD=Q329753|lt=Emperor Daigo}}'s princes caused fear of Michizane’s curse. The {{ill|Seiryōden lightning strike incident|ja|清涼殿落雷事件|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Seiryōden lightning strike incident|qq=}} further linked Michizane’s spirit with the thunder god. The court pardoned Michizane’s alleged crimes and posthumously elevated his Rank. In Kyoto’s {{ill|Kitano|ja|北野 (京都市)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kitano|1=Kitano|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11404253}}, Karatanojin, a local {{ill|Landlord deity|en|Landlord deity|WD=Q4160872|lt=landlord deity}}, was enshrined as part of {{ill|Kitano Tenmangū|en|Kitano Tenmangū|WD=Q662176|lt=Kitano Tenmangū}} to appease Michizane’s spirit.<ref>Karatanojin is now enshrined at the Hi no Miko Shrine within Kitano Tenmangū.</ref> Similarly, in Dazaifu, Michizane was worshipped at a mausoleum later developed into {{ill|Dazaifu Tenmangū|en|Dazaifu Tenmangū|WD=Q710786|lt=Dazaifu Tenmangū}} by order of Emperor Daigo and {{ill|Fujiwara_no_Nakahira|en|Fujiwara_no_Nakahira|WD=Q3090646|lt=Fujiwara no Nakahira}}. Additionally, {{ill|Osaka Tenmangū|en|Osaka Tenmangū|WD=Q385793|lt=Osaka Tenmangū}} was built in 949 after seven pine trees mysteriously sprouted overnight at a site believed to be under Michizane’s protection.
During the late Heian and Kamakura periods, the belief in Michizane’s wrath waned. According to the ''Tenjin Engi'' (天神縁起), he became revered as a merciful deity of integrity, poetry, and the arts, granting longevity in this life and bliss in the next.<ref>As Michizane was identified with the Eleven-Faced Kannon Bosatsu.</ref> Over time, Tenjin also became a protector against maritime disasters and was worshiped by royalty, military leaders, and merchants as a deity for victory and national protection. In the Edo period, Michizane was popularly venerated as the god of learning due to his scholarly achievements.
== Origin == {{ill|Kitano Tenmangū|en|Kitano Tenmangū|WD=Q662176|lt=Kitano Tenmangū}} and {{ill|Dazaifu Tenmangū|en|Dazaifu Tenmangū|WD=Q710786|lt=Dazaifu Tenmangū}} were established independently, each claiming to be the birthplace of Tenjin Faith. Others, such as {{ill|Hōfu Tenmangū|en|Hōfu Tenmangū|WD=Q704898|lt=Hōfu Tenmangū}} and {{ill|Yoki Tenman Shrine|ja|與喜天満神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yoki Tenman Shrine|qq=}}, also assert being the oldest origin sites.
== Local Tenjin Faith == [[Image:MATSUYAMA_CASTLE(IYO),TENJIN_YAGURA.JPG|thumb|right|250px|The Tenjin Yagura at Matsuyama Castle, Iyo Province]] [[Image:Japanese crest Kaga Umebachi.svg|thumb|right|150px|The Kaga Umebachi crest of the Maeda clan]]
; Hokuriku In {{ill|Fukui Prefecture|en|Fukui Prefecture|WD=Q133879|lt=Fukui Prefecture}} and {{ill|Toyama Prefecture|en|Toyama Prefecture|WD=Q132929|lt=Toyama Prefecture}}, Tenjin images are displayed in the alcove when a firstborn son is born. The tradition of offering flounder on January 25 is also observed. These practices were promoted by {{ill|Matsudaira Yoshinaga|en|Matsudaira Yoshinaga|WD=Q910802|lt=Matsudaira Yoshinaga}} during the late Edo period.
== Tenjin and the Number 25 == Michizane’s birth and death anniversaries fall on the 25th of June and February, respectively, leading to monthly festivals on the 25th. This association persists even today with events like the {{ill|Hajime Tenjin|ja|始め天神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hajime Tenjin|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} on January 25 and the {{ill|Shimai Tenjin|ja|終い天神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shimai Tenjin|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} on December 25.
== Tenjin and Oxen == Numerous legends link Michizane with oxen, portraying them as his loyal protectors and companions. Due to these stories, oxen are considered messengers of the deity and are often depicted in Tenmangū shrines as reclining ox statues.
== References == {{Reflist|2}}
